docs.title: ML Odyssey API Reference
docs.text: Comprehensive API documentation for ML Odyssey's tensor library and neural network components.

docs.title: Quick Navigation
docs.text:
• AnyTensor - Core tensor class with autograd support
  • Operations - Tensor operations (arithmetic, reduction, linear algebra)
  • Neural Networks - Layers, activations, and losses
  • Autograd - Automatic differentiation system
  • Training - Optimizers, schedulers, and data loading

docs.title: AnyTensor
docs.text:
The AnyTensor struct is the fundamental data structure in ML Odyssey. It supports:

  • Multi-dimensional arrays with arbitrary shape
  • Multiple data types (float32, float16, bfloat16, int8, etc.)
  • Automatic gradient computation via tape-based autograd
  • SIMD-optimized operations
from odyssey.core import AnyTensor, zeros, ones, randn

# Create tensors
var x = zeros[DType.float32](2, 3)        # Shape: (2, 3)
var y = ones[DType.float32](2, 3)         # Shape: (2, 3)
var z = randn[DType.float32](64, 128)     # Random normal

# Basic operations
var sum_result = x + y
var product = x * y
var matmul_result = x @ y.T               # Matrix multiplication

docs.title: Automatic Differentiation
docs.text:
ML Odyssey uses tape-based automatic differentiation for computing gradients:

from odyssey.autograd import Tape, no_grad

# Record operations on tape
var tape = Tape()
with tape:
    var x = randn[DType.float32](10, 5)
    var w = randn[DType.float32](5, 3)
    var y = x @ w
    var loss = y.sum()

# Compute gradients
var grads = tape.backward(loss)
var dx = grads.get(x)
var dw = grads.get(w)

docs.title: Layers and Models
docs.text:
Build neural networks using composable layers:

from odyssey.core.layers import Linear, Conv2d, BatchNorm2d, ReLU

# Define layers
var linear = Linear(784, 128)
var conv = Conv2d(3, 64, kernel_size=3, padding=1)
var bn = BatchNorm2d(64)
var relu = ReLU()

# Forward pass
var x = randn[DType.float32](32, 3, 28, 28)  # NCHW format
var out = conv.forward(x)
out = bn.forward(out)
out = relu.forward(out)

docs.title: Module Structure
docs.text:
src/odyssey/
├── core/                    # Core tensor library
│   ├── any_tensor.mojo       # AnyTensor struct
│   ├── arithmetic.mojo     # +, -, *, /, etc.
│   ├── reduction.mojo      # sum, mean, max, min
│   ├── shape.mojo          # reshape, transpose, view
│   ├── linalg.mojo         # matmul, dot, etc.
│   └── layers/             # Neural network layers
│       ├── linear.mojo
│       ├── conv2d.mojo
│       └── ...
├── autograd/               # Automatic differentiation
│   ├── tape.mojo           # Tape-based autograd
│   └── operations.mojo     # Differentiable ops
└── training/               # Training infrastructure
    ├── optimizers/         # SGD, Adam, AdamW, etc.
    ├── schedulers/         # Learning rate schedulers
    └── metrics/            # Accuracy, loss tracking

docs.title: Data Types
docs.text:
ML Odyssey supports multiple data types for tensors:

Type Description Use Case DType.float32 32-bit floating point Default, general training DType.float16 16-bit floating point Mixed precision training DType.bfloat16 Brain floating point TPU compatibility DType.int8 8-bit integer Quantized inference DType.int32 32-bit integer Indices, counters

docs.title: Memory Layout
docs.text:
Tensors use row-major (C-contiguous) memory layout by default. Key points:

  • Contiguous tensors: Optimal for SIMD operations
  • Strided tensors: Created by slicing, transpose; may require .contiguous()
  • Broadcasting: Automatic shape expansion following NumPy rules

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text:
ML Odyssey uses explicit error handling:

# Shape mismatch errors
var a = zeros[DType.float32](3, 4)
var b = zeros[DType.float32](5, 6)
var c = a + b  # Raises: "Cannot broadcast shapes [3, 4] and [5, 6]"

# Type mismatch errors
var x = zeros[DType.float32](3, 3)
var y = zeros[DType.int32](3, 3)
var z = x + y  # Raises: "DType mismatch: float32 vs int32"

docs.title: Performance Tips
docs.text:
1. Use contiguous tensors for SIMD optimization
  2. Batch operations instead of iterating over elements
  3. Enable mixed precision for memory/speed tradeoffs
  4. Profile with benchmarks in benchmarks/ directory

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• AnyTensor Reference - Complete tensor API
  • Operations Guide - Arithmetic operations
  • Building Models - Neural network layers
  • Training Guide - Optimization algorithms

docs.title: AnyTensor
docs.text: The core tensor class in ML Odyssey. Provides a dynamic, multi-dimensional array with automatic gradient computation support.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
from odyssey.core import AnyTensor

AnyTensor is a dynamic tensor supporting:

  • Arbitrary dimensions (0D scalars to N-D tensors)
  • Multiple data types (float32, float16, bfloat16, int8, etc.)
  • NumPy-style broadcasting
  • Memory-safe reference counting

docs.title: zeros
docs.text:
Create a tensor filled with zeros.

fn zeros(shape: List[Int], dtype: DType = DType.float32) raises -> AnyTensor
fn zeros[dtype: DType](dims: Int...) raises -> AnyTensor  # Variadic version

Parameters:

  • shape: List of dimension sizes
  • dtype: Data type (default: float32)

Example:

from odyssey.core import zeros

var a = zeros[DType.float32](3, 4)       # Shape: (3, 4)
var b = zeros(List[Int](2, 3, 4), DType.float16)  # Shape: (2, 3, 4)

docs.title: ones
docs.text:
Create a tensor filled with ones.

fn ones(shape: List[Int], dtype: DType = DType.float32) raises -> AnyTensor
fn ones[dtype: DType](dims: Int...) raises -> AnyTensor

Example:

from odyssey.core import ones

var x = ones[DType.float32](64, 128)

docs.title: full
docs.text:
Create a tensor filled with a specified value.

fn full(shape: List[Int], fill_value: Scalar, dtype: DType) raises -> AnyTensor

Parameters:

  • shape: List of dimension sizes
  • fill_value: Value to fill the tensor with
  • dtype: Data type

Example:

from odyssey.core import full

var x = full(List[Int](3, 3), 3.14, DType.float32)

docs.title: randn
docs.text:
Create a tensor with random values from a normal distribution.

fn randn(shape: List[Int], dtype: DType = DType.float32, seed: Int = -1) raises -> AnyTensor
fn randn[dtype: DType](dims: Int...) raises -> AnyTensor

Parameters:

  • shape: List of dimension sizes
  • dtype: Data type (default: float32)
  • seed: Random seed for reproducibility (-1 for random)

Example:

from odyssey.core import randn

var x = randn[DType.float32](32, 784)  # Random input batch
var w = randn[DType.float32](784, 128, seed=42)  # Reproducible weights

docs.title: arange
docs.text:
Create a 1D tensor with evenly spaced values.

fn arange(start: Scalar, stop: Scalar, step: Scalar, dtype: DType) raises -> AnyTensor

Parameters:

  • start: Start value (inclusive)
  • stop: End value (exclusive)
  • step: Step size between values
  • dtype: Data type

Example:

from odyssey.core import arange

var x = arange(0.0, 10.0, 1.0, DType.float32)  # [0, 1, 2, ..., 9]
var y = arange(0.0, 1.0, 0.1, DType.float32)   # [0, 0.1, 0.2, ..., 0.9]

docs.title: eye
docs.text:
Create an identity matrix.

fn eye(n: Int, dtype: DType = DType.float32) raises -> AnyTensor

Parameters:

  • n: Size of the square identity matrix
  • dtype: Data type (default: float32)

Example:

from odyssey.core import eye

var I = eye(3)  # 3x3 identity matrix

docs.title: linspace
docs.text:
Create a 1D tensor with linearly spaced values.

fn linspace(start: Scalar, stop: Scalar, num: Int, dtype: DType) raises -> AnyTensor

Parameters:

  • start: Start value
  • stop: End value (inclusive)
  • num: Number of values to generate
  • dtype: Data type

Example:

from odyssey.core import linspace

var x = linspace(0.0, 1.0, 11, DType.float32)  # [0, 0.1, 0.2, ..., 1.0]

docs.title: shape
docs.text:
Get the shape of the tensor.

fn shape(self) -> List[Int]

Example:

var x = zeros[DType.float32](3, 4, 5)
print(x.shape())  # [3, 4, 5]

docs.title: dtype
docs.text:
Get the data type of the tensor.

fn dtype(self) -> DType

Example:

var x = zeros[DType.float16](3, 4)
print(x.dtype())  # float16

docs.title: numel
docs.text:
Get the total number of elements.

fn numel(self) -> Int

Example:

var x = zeros[DType.float32](3, 4, 5)
print(x.numel())  # 60

docs.title: ndim
docs.text:
Get the number of dimensions.

fn ndim(self) -> Int

Example:

var x = zeros[DType.float32](3, 4, 5)
print(x.ndim())  # 3

docs.title: size
docs.text:
Get the size of a specific dimension.

fn size(self, dim: Int) -> Int

Parameters:

  • dim: Dimension index (supports negative indexing)

Example:

var x = zeros[DType.float32](3, 4, 5)
print(x.size(0))   # 3
print(x.size(-1))  # 5

docs.title: is_contiguous
docs.text:
Check if the tensor is contiguous in memory.

fn is_contiguous(self) -> Bool

Example:

var x = zeros[DType.float32](3, 4)
print(x.is_contiguous())  # True

var y = x.T  # Transpose creates non-contiguous view
print(y.is_contiguous())  # False

docs.title: item
docs.text:
Get a scalar value from a single-element tensor.

fn item[dtype: DType](self) -> Scalar[dtype]

Example:

var x = full(List[Int](1), 3.14, DType.float32)
var value = x.item[DType.float32]()  # 3.14

docs.title: __getitem__
docs.text:
Access elements using indexing.

fn __getitem__(self, indices: List[Int]) raises -> AnyTensor

Example:

var x = randn[DType.float32](3, 4)
var row = x[List[Int](0)]  # First row
var elem = x[List[Int](1, 2)]  # Element at (1, 2)

docs.title: reshape
docs.text:
Reshape tensor to new dimensions.

fn reshape(self, new_shape: List[Int]) raises -> AnyTensor

Parameters:

  • new_shape: New shape (must have same total elements)

Example:

var x = arange(0.0, 12.0, 1.0, DType.float32)  # Shape: (12,)
var y = x.reshape(List[Int](3, 4))             # Shape: (3, 4)
var z = y.reshape(List[Int](2, 6))             # Shape: (2, 6)

docs.title: transpose / T
docs.text:
Transpose the tensor.

fn transpose(self) raises -> AnyTensor
fn T(self) raises -> AnyTensor  # Alias

Example:

var x = randn[DType.float32](3, 4)
var y = x.T  # Shape: (4, 3)

docs.title: squeeze
docs.text:
Remove dimensions of size 1.

fn squeeze(self, dim: Optional[Int] = None) raises -> AnyTensor

Parameters:

  • dim: Dimension to squeeze (None for all)

Example:

var x = zeros[DType.float32](1, 3, 1, 4)
var y = x.squeeze()          # Shape: (3, 4)
var z = x.squeeze(dim=0)     # Shape: (3, 1, 4)

docs.title: unsqueeze
docs.text:
Add a dimension of size 1.

fn unsqueeze(self, dim: Int) raises -> AnyTensor

Parameters:

  • dim: Position to insert new dimension

Example:

var x = zeros[DType.float32](3, 4)
var y = x.unsqueeze(0)   # Shape: (1, 3, 4)
var z = x.unsqueeze(-1)  # Shape: (3, 4, 1)

docs.title: contiguous
docs.text:
Return a contiguous copy of the tensor.

fn contiguous(self) raises -> AnyTensor

Example:

var x = randn[DType.float32](4, 3)
var y = x.T  # Non-contiguous view
var z = y.contiguous()  # Contiguous copy

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Arithmetic Operations
  • Reduction Operations
  • Linear Algebra
  • Indexing and Slicing

docs.title: Automatic Differentiation
docs.text: Tape-based automatic differentiation for gradient computation.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
ML Odyssey uses tape-based (dynamic) automatic differentiation:

  1. Operations are recorded on a "tape" during the forward pass
  2. Gradients are computed by replaying the tape in reverse

docs.title: Tape
docs.text:
The Tape class records operations for gradient computation.

from odyssey.autograd import Tape

docs.title: Creating a Tape
docs.text: var tape = Tape()

docs.title: Recording Operations
docs.text:
Use a context manager to record operations:

var tape = Tape()
with tape:
    var x = randn[DType.float32](10, 5)
    var w = randn[DType.float32](5, 3)
    var y = x @ w
    var loss = y.sum()

Or explicitly start/stop recording:

var tape = Tape()
tape.start_recording()

var x = randn[DType.float32](10, 5)
var w = randn[DType.float32](5, 3)
var y = x @ w
var loss = y.sum()

tape.stop_recording()

docs.title: Computing Gradients
docs.text:
# Compute gradients with respect to all recorded tensors
var grads = tape.backward(loss)

# Access individual gradients
var dx = grads.get(x)  # Gradient w.r.t x
var dw = grads.get(w)  # Gradient w.r.t w

docs.title: Complete Example
docs.text:
from odyssey.autograd import Tape
from odyssey.core import randn

fn train_step():
    # Create input and weights
    var x = randn[DType.float32](32, 784)
    var w = randn[DType.float32](784, 10)
    var b = randn[DType.float32](10)

    # Record forward pass
    var tape = Tape()
    with tape:
        var logits = x @ w + b
        var probs = softmax(logits, dim=-1)
        var loss = cross_entropy_loss(probs, targets)

    # Compute gradients
    var grads = tape.backward(loss)

    # Update weights
    var dw = grads.get(w)
    var db = grads.get(b)
    w = w - learning_rate * dw
    b = b - learning_rate * db

docs.title: no_grad
docs.text:
Disable gradient computation for efficiency.

from odyssey.autograd import no_grad

Context Manager:

with no_grad():
    # No gradients computed here
    var output = model.forward(input)
    var predictions = output.argmax(dim=-1)

Use Cases:

  • Inference (no training)
  • Validation during training
  • Computing metrics
  • Preprocessing

docs.title: is_grad_enabled
docs.text:
Check if gradients are currently enabled.

from odyssey.autograd import is_grad_enabled

if is_grad_enabled():
    print("Gradients are being tracked")

docs.title: set_grad_enabled
docs.text:
Programmatically enable/disable gradients.

from odyssey.autograd import set_grad_enabled

set_grad_enabled(False)  # Disable
# ... operations without gradients ...
set_grad_enabled(True)   # Re-enable

docs.title: Gradient Accumulation
docs.text:
For large batch training with limited memory:

var tape = Tape()
var accumulation_steps = 4

for i in range(accumulation_steps):
    var batch = get_batch(i)

    with tape:
        var output = model.forward(batch.input)
        var loss = criterion.forward(output, batch.target)
        loss = loss / accumulation_steps  # Scale for accumulation

    var grads = tape.backward(loss)
    accumulate_gradients(grads)  # Add to running total

# Apply accumulated gradients
optimizer.step()
optimizer.zero_grad()

docs.title: Retain Graph
docs.text:
By default, the computation graph is freed after backward(). Use retain_graph=True for multiple backward passes:

var grads1 = tape.backward(loss1, retain_graph=True)
var grads2 = tape.backward(loss2)  # Reuses same graph

Use Cases:

  • Multiple losses
  • Higher-order gradients
  • Gradient penalty computation

docs.title: Supported Operations
docs.text: The following operations are differentiable:

docs.title: Arithmetic
docs.text:
• Addition (+)
  • Subtraction (-)
  • Multiplication (*)
  • Division (/)
  • Power (**)
  • Negation (-)

docs.title: Matrix Operations
docs.text:
• Matrix multiplication (@, matmul)
  • Transpose (T)
  • Dot product (dot)

docs.title: Reductions
docs.text:
• Sum (sum)
  • Mean (mean)
  • Max (max)

docs.title: Activations
docs.text:
• ReLU, LeakyReLU, ELU, GELU
  • Sigmoid, Tanh
  • Softmax, LogSoftmax

docs.title: Layers
docs.text:
• Linear, Conv2d
  • BatchNorm2d
  • MaxPool2d, AvgPool2d
  • Dropout

docs.title: Losses
docs.text:
• CrossEntropyLoss
  • MSELoss
  • BCELoss

docs.title: Gradient Checkpointing
docs.text:
Save memory by recomputing forward pass during backward:

from odyssey.autograd import checkpoint

fn expensive_forward(x: AnyTensor) -> AnyTensor:
    # Memory-intensive operations
    ...

# Checkpointed call - forward recomputed during backward
var output = checkpoint(expensive_forward, input)

Trade-off:

  • Lower memory usage
  • Higher computation time

docs.title: Check for NaN/Inf
docs.text:
var grads = tape.backward(loss)
for tensor_id, grad in grads.items():
    if grad.has_nan():
        print("NaN gradient detected!")
    if grad.has_inf():
        print("Inf gradient detected!")

docs.title: Gradient Clipping
docs.text:
Prevent exploding gradients:

from odyssey.training import clip_grad_norm

# Clip by global norm
clip_grad_norm(model.parameters(), max_norm=1.0)

# Or clip by value
clip_grad_value(model.parameters(), clip_value=0.5)

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• AnyTensor Reference - Core tensor class
  • Optimizers - Apply gradients
  • Layers - Neural network layers

docs.title: Activation Functions
docs.text: Non-linear activation functions for neural networks.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Activation functions introduce non-linearity into neural networks, enabling them to learn complex patterns. All activations support both functional and layer forms.

docs.title: ReLU
docs.text:
Rectified Linear Unit: max(0, x)

from odyssey.core import relu
from odyssey.core.layers import ReLU

Functional:

var y = relu(x)

Layer:

var activation = ReLU()
var y = activation.forward(x)

Properties:

  • Fast to compute
  • Sparse activations (zeros for negative inputs)
  • Can cause "dying ReLU" problem

docs.title: LeakyReLU
docs.text:
Leaky ReLU: max(alpha*x, x) where alpha is small (default 0.01)

from odyssey.core import leaky_relu
from odyssey.core.layers import LeakyReLU

# Functional
var y = leaky_relu(x, alpha=0.01)

# Layer
var activation = LeakyReLU(negative_slope=0.01)
var y = activation.forward(x)

Parameters:

  • negative_slope / alpha: Slope for negative values (default: 0.01)

docs.title: ELU
docs.text:
Exponential Linear Unit.

from odyssey.core import elu
from odyssey.core.layers import ELU

# Functional
var y = elu(x, alpha=1.0)

# Layer
var activation = ELU(alpha=1.0)
var y = activation.forward(x)

Formula:

  • x if x > 0
  • alpha * (exp(x) - 1) if x <= 0

docs.title: GELU
docs.text:
Gaussian Error Linear Unit (used in transformers).

from odyssey.core import gelu
from odyssey.core.layers import GELU

var y = gelu(x)

Formula: x * Phi(x) where Phi is the Gaussian CDF

Properties:

  • Smooth, differentiable everywhere
  • Default activation in BERT, GPT models

docs.title: SiLU / Swish
docs.text:
Sigmoid Linear Unit: x * sigmoid(x)

from odyssey.core import silu
from odyssey.core.layers import SiLU

var y = silu(x)

Properties:

  • Self-gated activation
  • Smooth and non-monotonic

docs.title: Sigmoid
docs.text:
Logistic sigmoid: 1 / (1 + exp(-x))

from odyssey.core import sigmoid
from odyssey.core.layers import Sigmoid

# Functional
var y = sigmoid(x)

# Layer
var activation = Sigmoid()
var y = activation.forward(x)

Properties:

  • Output range: (0, 1)
  • Used for binary classification outputs
  • Can suffer from vanishing gradients

docs.title: Tanh
docs.text:
Hyperbolic tangent: (exp(x) - exp(-x)) / (exp(x) + exp(-x))

from odyssey.core import tanh
from odyssey.core.layers import Tanh

var y = tanh(x)

Properties:

  • Output range: (-1, 1)
  • Zero-centered outputs
  • Used in RNNs and LSTMs

docs.title: Softmax
docs.text:
Normalize to probability distribution: exp(x_i) / sum(exp(x_j))

from odyssey.core import softmax
from odyssey.core.layers import Softmax

# Along last dimension (default)
var probs = softmax(logits, dim=-1)

# Layer form
var activation = Softmax(dim=-1)
var probs = activation.forward(logits)

Parameters:

  • dim: Dimension to normalize over (default: -1)

Properties:

  • Outputs sum to 1.0 along specified dimension
  • Used for multi-class classification
  • Numerically stable implementation (log-sum-exp trick)

docs.title: LogSoftmax
docs.text:
Log of softmax: log(softmax(x))

from odyssey.core import log_softmax
from odyssey.core.layers import LogSoftmax

var log_probs = log_softmax(logits, dim=-1)

Properties:

  • More numerically stable than log(softmax(x))
  • Often used with NLLLoss

docs.title: Comparison Table
docs.text: Activation Range Properties Use Case ReLU [0, inf) Fast, sparse Default for hidden layers LeakyReLU (-inf, inf) Avoids dying ReLU Alternative to ReLU ELU (-alpha, inf) Smooth negative region Alternative to ReLU GELU (-0.17, inf) Smooth, probabilistic Transformers SiLU (-0.28, inf) Self-gated Modern architectures Sigmoid (0, 1) Bounded, smooth Binary outputs Tanh (-1, 1) Zero-centered RNNs, LSTMs Softmax (0, 1) Sums to 1 Classification output

docs.title: Gradient Properties
docs.text:
All activations support backward pass for gradient computation:

# Forward pass
var y = relu(x)

# Backward pass (during training)
var grad_x = relu_backward(grad_y, x)

ReLU Gradient:

  • 1 if x > 0
  • 0 if x <= 0

Sigmoid Gradient:

  • sigmoid(x) * (1 - sigmoid(x))

Tanh Gradient:

  • 1 - tanh(x)^2

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Use ReLU as default for hidden layers
  2. Use GELU for transformer models
  3. Use Softmax for classification outputs
  4. Use Sigmoid for binary classification
  5. Avoid Sigmoid/Tanh in deep networks (vanishing gradients)

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Layers - Neural network layers
  • Losses - Loss functions
  • AnyTensor Reference - Core tensor class

docs.title: Neural Network Layers
docs.text: Building blocks for constructing neural network models.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
All layers follow a consistent interface:

trait Layer:
    fn forward(mut self, x: AnyTensor) raises -> AnyTensor
    fn backward(mut self, grad_output: AnyTensor) raises -> AnyTensor
    fn parameters(self) -> List[AnyTensor]

docs.title: Linear
docs.text:
Fully connected layer: y = xW^T + b

from odyssey.core.layers import Linear

Constructor:

fn __init__(out self, in_features: Int, out_features: Int, bias: Bool = True) raises

Parameters:

  • in_features: Size of input features
  • out_features: Size of output features
  • bias: Include bias term (default: True)

Example:

from odyssey.core.layers import Linear
from odyssey.core import randn

var linear = Linear(784, 128)  # 784 -> 128
var x = randn[DType.float32](32, 784)  # Batch of 32
var y = linear.forward(x)  # Shape: (32, 128)

docs.title: Conv2d
docs.text:
2D convolution layer for image processing.

from odyssey.core.layers import Conv2d

Constructor:

fn __init__(
    out self,
    in_channels: Int,
    out_channels: Int,
    kernel_size: Int,
    stride: Int = 1,
    padding: Int = 0,
    dilation: Int = 1,
    groups: Int = 1,
    bias: Bool = True,
) raises

Parameters:

  • in_channels: Number of input channels
  • out_channels: Number of output channels (filters)
  • kernel_size: Size of the convolving kernel
  • stride: Stride of the convolution (default: 1)
  • padding: Zero-padding added to input (default: 0)
  • dilation: Spacing between kernel elements (default: 1)
  • groups: Number of blocked connections (default: 1)
  • bias: Include bias term (default: True)

Example:

from odyssey.core.layers import Conv2d
from odyssey.core import randn

# 3 input channels, 64 output channels, 3x3 kernel
var conv = Conv2d(3, 64, kernel_size=3, padding=1)

# Input: NCHW format (batch, channels, height, width)
var x = randn[DType.float32](32, 3, 28, 28)
var y = conv.forward(x)  # Shape: (32, 64, 28, 28)

Output Size Formula:

H_out = floor((H_in + 2*padding - dilation*(kernel_size-1) - 1) / stride + 1)
W_out = floor((W_in + 2*padding - dilation*(kernel_size-1) - 1) / stride + 1)

docs.title: BatchNorm2d
docs.text:
2D batch normalization for convolutional layers.

from odyssey.core.layers import BatchNorm2d

Constructor:

fn __init__(
    out self,
    num_features: Int,
    eps: Float = 1e-5,
    momentum: Float = 0.1,
    affine: Bool = True,
) raises

Parameters:

  • num_features: Number of channels
  • eps: Small value for numerical stability (default: 1e-5)
  • momentum: Running stats momentum (default: 0.1)
  • affine: Learnable scale and shift (default: True)

Example:

from odyssey.core.layers import BatchNorm2d, Conv2d
from odyssey.core import randn

var conv = Conv2d(3, 64, kernel_size=3, padding=1)
var bn = BatchNorm2d(64)

var x = randn[DType.float32](32, 3, 28, 28)
var y = conv.forward(x)
y = bn.forward(y)  # Normalized output

Training vs Inference:

# Training mode (default) - updates running stats
bn.train()
y = bn.forward(x)

# Inference mode - uses running stats
bn.set_inference_mode()
y = bn.forward(x)

docs.title: MaxPool2d
docs.text:
2D max pooling layer.

from odyssey.core.layers import MaxPool2d

Constructor:

fn __init__(
    out self,
    kernel_size: Int,
    stride: Optional[Int] = None,
    padding: Int = 0,
) raises

Parameters:

  • kernel_size: Size of the pooling window
  • stride: Stride of pooling (default: same as kernel_size)
  • padding: Zero-padding (default: 0)

Example:

from odyssey.core.layers import MaxPool2d
from odyssey.core import randn

var pool = MaxPool2d(kernel_size=2, stride=2)
var x = randn[DType.float32](32, 64, 28, 28)
var y = pool.forward(x)  # Shape: (32, 64, 14, 14)

docs.title: AvgPool2d
docs.text:
2D average pooling layer.

from odyssey.core.layers import AvgPool2d

var pool = AvgPool2d(kernel_size=2, stride=2)
var x = randn[DType.float32](32, 64, 28, 28)
var y = pool.forward(x)  # Shape: (32, 64, 14, 14)

docs.title: AdaptiveAvgPool2d
docs.text:
Adaptive average pooling to target output size.

from odyssey.core.layers import AdaptiveAvgPool2d

var pool = AdaptiveAvgPool2d(output_size=(1, 1))  # Global average pooling
var x = randn[DType.float32](32, 512, 7, 7)
var y = pool.forward(x)  # Shape: (32, 512, 1, 1)

docs.title: Dropout
docs.text:
Randomly zero elements during training.

from odyssey.core.layers import Dropout

Constructor:

fn __init__(out self, p: Float = 0.5) raises

Parameters:

  • p: Probability of dropping an element (default: 0.5)

Example:

from odyssey.core.layers import Dropout
from odyssey.core import randn

var dropout = Dropout(p=0.5)

# Training mode - applies dropout
dropout.train()
var x = randn[DType.float32](32, 128)
var y = dropout.forward(x)  # ~50% of values are zero, rest scaled by 2

# Inference mode - no dropout
dropout.set_inference_mode()
y = dropout.forward(x)  # Identity function

docs.title: Embedding
docs.text:
Lookup table for discrete tokens.

from odyssey.core.layers import Embedding

Constructor:

fn __init__(out self, num_embeddings: Int, embedding_dim: Int) raises

Parameters:

  • num_embeddings: Size of vocabulary
  • embedding_dim: Dimension of embedding vectors

Example:

from odyssey.core.layers import Embedding
from odyssey.core import zeros

var embed = Embedding(10000, 256)  # 10K vocab, 256-dim embeddings

# Input: token indices
var tokens = zeros[DType.int32](32, 100)  # 32 sequences, 100 tokens each
var embeddings = embed.forward(tokens)  # Shape: (32, 100, 256)

docs.title: Flatten
docs.text:
Flatten all dimensions except batch.

from odyssey.core.layers import Flatten

var flatten = Flatten()
var x = randn[DType.float32](32, 64, 7, 7)
var y = flatten.forward(x)  # Shape: (32, 3136)

docs.title: Sequential Container
docs.text:
Combine layers into a sequential model.

from odyssey.core.layers import Sequential, Linear, ReLU, Dropout

var model = Sequential()
model.add(Linear(784, 256))
model.add(ReLU())
model.add(Dropout(0.5))
model.add(Linear(256, 10))

var x = randn[DType.float32](32, 784)
var y = model.forward(x)  # Shape: (32, 10)

docs.title: Getting Parameters
docs.text:
# Get all trainable parameters
var params = layer.parameters()

# Count parameters
var total = 0
for p in params:
    total += p.numel()
print("Total parameters:", total)

docs.title: Training Mode
docs.text:
# Set to training mode
layer.train()

# Set to inference mode
layer.set_inference_mode()

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Activations - Activation functions
  • Losses - Loss functions
  • Optimizers - Parameter optimization

docs.title: Loss Functions
docs.text: Loss functions for training neural networks.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Loss functions measure the discrepancy between predicted and target values. All loss functions support both functional and class forms.

docs.title: CrossEntropyLoss
docs.text:
Combines LogSoftmax and NLLLoss for multi-class classification.

from odyssey.core import cross_entropy_loss
from odyssey.core.layers import CrossEntropyLoss

Functional:

# logits: (batch, num_classes)
# targets: (batch,) - class indices
var loss = cross_entropy_loss(logits, targets)

Class Form:

var criterion = CrossEntropyLoss()
var loss = criterion.forward(logits, targets)

Parameters:

  • weight: Optional class weights for imbalanced datasets
  • reduction: "mean" (default), "sum", or "none"
  • label_smoothing: Smooth targets (default: 0.0)

Example:

from odyssey.core import randn, zeros
from odyssey.core.layers import CrossEntropyLoss

var logits = randn[DType.float32](32, 10)  # 32 samples, 10 classes
var targets = zeros[DType.int32](32)  # Class indices

var criterion = CrossEntropyLoss()
var loss = criterion.forward(logits, targets)
print("Loss:", loss.item[DType.float32]())

docs.title: NLLLoss
docs.text:
Negative Log Likelihood Loss (expects log-probabilities).

from odyssey.core import nll_loss
from odyssey.core.layers import NLLLoss

# log_probs: (batch, num_classes) - output of LogSoftmax
# targets: (batch,) - class indices
var loss = nll_loss(log_probs, targets)

Use with LogSoftmax:

var log_probs = log_softmax(logits, dim=-1)
var loss = nll_loss(log_probs, targets)

docs.title: BinaryCrossEntropyLoss
docs.text:
Binary cross-entropy for binary classification.

from odyssey.core import binary_cross_entropy
from odyssey.core.layers import BCELoss

# predictions: (batch,) - probabilities in [0, 1]
# targets: (batch,) - binary labels (0 or 1)
var loss = binary_cross_entropy(predictions, targets)

With Logits (more stable):

from odyssey.core import binary_cross_entropy_with_logits
from odyssey.core.layers import BCEWithLogitsLoss

# logits: (batch,) - raw scores
# targets: (batch,) - binary labels
var loss = binary_cross_entropy_with_logits(logits, targets)

docs.title: MSELoss
docs.text:
Mean Squared Error: mean((pred - target)^2)

from odyssey.core import mse_loss
from odyssey.core.layers import MSELoss

Functional:

var loss = mse_loss(predictions, targets)

Class Form:

var criterion = MSELoss(reduction="mean")
var loss = criterion.forward(predictions, targets)

Parameters:

  • reduction: "mean" (default), "sum", or "none"

Example:

from odyssey.core import randn
from odyssey.core.layers import MSELoss

var predictions = randn[DType.float32](32, 10)
var targets = randn[DType.float32](32, 10)

var criterion = MSELoss()
var loss = criterion.forward(predictions, targets)

docs.title: L1Loss
docs.text:
Mean Absolute Error: mean(|pred - target|)

from odyssey.core import l1_loss
from odyssey.core.layers import L1Loss

var loss = l1_loss(predictions, targets)

Properties:

  • More robust to outliers than MSE
  • Less smooth gradient at zero

docs.title: SmoothL1Loss
docs.text:
Huber Loss: combines L1 and L2.

from odyssey.core import smooth_l1_loss
from odyssey.core.layers import SmoothL1Loss

var criterion = SmoothL1Loss(beta=1.0)
var loss = criterion.forward(predictions, targets)

Formula:

  • 0.5 * (x)^2 / beta if |x| < beta
  • |x| - 0.5 * beta otherwise

Properties:

  • Smooth gradient everywhere
  • Less sensitive to outliers than MSE

docs.title: TripletMarginLoss
docs.text:
Triplet loss for metric learning.

from odyssey.core.layers import TripletMarginLoss

var criterion = TripletMarginLoss(margin=1.0)
# anchor, positive, negative: (batch, embedding_dim)
var loss = criterion.forward(anchor, positive, negative)

Formula: max(0, d(a, p) - d(a, n) + margin)

docs.title: CosineEmbeddingLoss
docs.text:
Cosine similarity loss.

from odyssey.core.layers import CosineEmbeddingLoss

var criterion = CosineEmbeddingLoss(margin=0.0)
# x1, x2: (batch, embedding_dim)
# labels: (batch,) - 1 for similar, -1 for dissimilar
var loss = criterion.forward(x1, x2, labels)

docs.title: Reduction Modes
docs.text:
All losses support three reduction modes:

Mode Description Output Shape "mean" Average over all elements () scalar "sum" Sum over all elements () scalar "none" No reduction Same as input

Example:

# Per-sample losses (useful for weighted averaging)
var criterion = MSELoss(reduction="none")
var per_sample_loss = criterion.forward(pred, target)  # Shape: (batch,)

# Custom weighting
var weights = randn[DType.float32](batch_size)
var weighted_loss = (per_sample_loss * weights).mean()

docs.title: Class Weights
docs.text:
Handle imbalanced datasets with class weights:

# Higher weight for rare classes
var weights = full(List[Int](10), 1.0, DType.float32)
weights[List[Int](7)] = 5.0  # 5x weight for class 7

var criterion = CrossEntropyLoss(weight=weights)

docs.title: Label Smoothing
docs.text:
Regularize by softening hard targets:

# Instead of [0, 0, 1, 0] -> [0.025, 0.025, 0.925, 0.025]
var criterion = CrossEntropyLoss(label_smoothing=0.1)

Benefits:

  • Prevents overconfident predictions
  • Improves generalization

docs.title: Custom Loss Functions
docs.text:
Create custom losses by combining existing operations:

fn focal_loss(
    logits: AnyTensor,
    targets: AnyTensor,
    gamma: Float = 2.0,
    alpha: Float = 0.25,
) raises -> AnyTensor:
    """Focal loss for imbalanced classification."""
    var probs = softmax(logits, dim=-1)
    var ce = cross_entropy_loss(logits, targets, reduction="none")

    # Focus on hard examples
    var pt = gather(probs, dim=1, index=targets.unsqueeze(-1)).squeeze(-1)
    var focal_weight = alpha * (1 - pt) ** gamma

    return (focal_weight * ce).mean()

docs.title: Comparison Table
docs.text: Loss Use Case Input Type Output CrossEntropy Multi-class Logits + Class IDs Scalar NLLLoss Multi-class Log-probs + Class IDs Scalar BCELoss Binary Probabilities Scalar BCEWithLogits Binary Logits Scalar MSELoss Regression Continuous Scalar L1Loss Regression Continuous Scalar SmoothL1Loss Regression Continuous Scalar

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Layers - Neural network layers
  • Activations - Activation functions
  • Optimizers - Parameter optimization

docs.title: Arithmetic Operations
docs.text: Element-wise arithmetic operations on tensors with broadcasting support.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
All arithmetic operations support:

  • Broadcasting: Automatic shape expansion following NumPy rules
  • Type safety: Both operands must have the same dtype
  • Autograd: Gradients tracked when recorded on tape

docs.title: Addition (+)
docs.text:
Element-wise addition.

fn __add__(self, other: AnyTensor) raises -> AnyTensor
fn add(a: AnyTensor, b: AnyTensor) raises -> AnyTensor

Example:

from odyssey.core import zeros, ones, add

var a = ones[DType.float32](3, 4)
var b = ones[DType.float32](3, 4)
var c = a + b  # All elements are 2.0
var d = add(a, b)  # Same as a + b

Broadcasting:

var x = randn[DType.float32](3, 4)  # Shape: (3, 4)
var y = randn[DType.float32](4)     # Shape: (4,)
var z = x + y  # y broadcasted to (3, 4)

docs.title: Subtraction (-)
docs.text:
Element-wise subtraction.

fn __sub__(self, other: AnyTensor) raises -> AnyTensor
fn subtract(a: AnyTensor, b: AnyTensor) raises -> AnyTensor

Example:

var a = full(List[Int](3, 3), 5.0, DType.float32)
var b = ones[DType.float32](3, 3)
var c = a - b  # All elements are 4.0

docs.title: Multiplication (*)
docs.text:
Element-wise multiplication (Hadamard product).

fn __mul__(self, other: AnyTensor) raises -> AnyTensor
fn multiply(a: AnyTensor, b: AnyTensor) raises -> AnyTensor

Example:

var a = full(List[Int](2, 3), 2.0, DType.float32)
var b = full(List[Int](2, 3), 3.0, DType.float32)
var c = a * b  # All elements are 6.0

docs.title: Division (/)
docs.text:
Element-wise division.

fn __truediv__(self, other: AnyTensor) raises -> AnyTensor
fn divide(a: AnyTensor, b: AnyTensor) raises -> AnyTensor

Example:

var a = full(List[Int](2, 2), 10.0, DType.float32)
var b = full(List[Int](2, 2), 2.0, DType.float32)
var c = a / b  # All elements are 5.0

docs.title: Floor Division (//)
docs.text:
Element-wise floor division.

fn __floordiv__(self, other: AnyTensor) raises -> AnyTensor
fn floor_divide(a: AnyTensor, b: AnyTensor) raises -> AnyTensor

Example:

var a = full(List[Int](2, 2), 7.0, DType.float32)
var b = full(List[Int](2, 2), 2.0, DType.float32)
var c = a // b  # All elements are 3.0

docs.title: Modulo (%)
docs.text:
Element-wise modulo.

fn __mod__(self, other: AnyTensor) raises -> AnyTensor
fn modulo(a: AnyTensor, b: AnyTensor) raises -> AnyTensor

Example:

var a = full(List[Int](2, 2), 7.0, DType.float32)
var b = full(List[Int](2, 2), 3.0, DType.float32)
var c = a % b  # All elements are 1.0

docs.title: Power (**)
docs.text:
Element-wise exponentiation.

fn __pow__(self, other: AnyTensor) raises -> AnyTensor
fn power(a: AnyTensor, b: AnyTensor) raises -> AnyTensor

Example:

var a = full(List[Int](2, 2), 2.0, DType.float32)
var b = full(List[Int](2, 2), 3.0, DType.float32)
var c = a ** b  # All elements are 8.0

docs.title: Negation (-)
docs.text:
Element-wise negation.

fn __neg__(self) -> AnyTensor
fn negative(x: AnyTensor) raises -> AnyTensor

Example:

var a = ones[DType.float32](3, 3)
var b = -a  # All elements are -1.0

docs.title: Absolute Value
docs.text:
Element-wise absolute value.

fn abs(self) raises -> AnyTensor
fn absolute(x: AnyTensor) raises -> AnyTensor

Example:

var a = full(List[Int](2, 2), -3.0, DType.float32)
var b = a.abs()  # All elements are 3.0

docs.title: Scalar Operations
docs.text:
Operations with scalar values on the right-hand side.

var x = randn[DType.float32](3, 4)
var y = x + 1.0   # Add scalar
var z = x * 2.0   # Multiply by scalar
var w = x / 10.0  # Divide by scalar
var v = x ** 2.0  # Square all elements

docs.title: In-Place Operations
docs.text:
In-place operations modify the tensor directly (where supported).

fn iadd(mut self, other: AnyTensor) raises -> None
fn isub(mut self, other: AnyTensor) raises -> None
fn imul(mut self, other: AnyTensor) raises -> None
fn idiv(mut self, other: AnyTensor) raises -> None

Example:

var x = ones[DType.float32](3, 3)
x.iadd(ones[DType.float32](3, 3))  # x now contains 2.0

docs.title: Broadcasting Rules
docs.text:
Broadcasting automatically expands tensors to compatible shapes:

  1. Shapes are aligned from the trailing dimensions
  2. Dimensions of size 1 can broadcast to any size
  3. Missing dimensions are treated as size 1

Examples:

(3, 4) + (4,)     -> (3, 4)  # (4,) broadcasts to (1, 4) then (3, 4)
(3, 1) + (1, 4)   -> (3, 4)  # Both dimensions broadcast
(5, 3, 1) + (3, 4) -> (5, 3, 4)  # Leading dimension preserved

Invalid broadcasting:

(3, 4) + (5,)     -> Error!  # 4 != 5, cannot broadcast
(3, 4) + (4, 3)   -> Error!  # Incompatible shapes

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text:
Shape and type mismatches raise errors:

var a = zeros[DType.float32](3, 4)
var b = zeros[DType.float32](5, 6)
var c = a + b  # Raises: "Cannot broadcast shapes [3, 4] and [5, 6]"

var x = zeros[DType.float32](3, 3)
var y = zeros[DType.int32](3, 3)
var z = x + y  # Raises: "DType mismatch: float32 vs int32"

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Reduction Operations - Sum, mean, max, min
  • AnyTensor Reference - Core tensor class

docs.title: Indexing and Slicing
docs.text: Access and manipulate tensor elements and subarrays.

docs.title: Single Element Access
docs.text:
Access individual elements using a list of indices.

fn __getitem__(self, indices: List[Int]) raises -> AnyTensor

Example:

from odyssey.core import randn

var x = randn[DType.float32](3, 4, 5)

# Access single element (returns 0D tensor)
var elem = x[List[Int](0, 1, 2)]

# Access row
var row = x[List[Int](0)]  # Shape: (4, 5)

# Access slice along first two dimensions
var slice = x[List[Int](0, 1)]  # Shape: (5,)

docs.title: Setting Elements
docs.text:
Set individual elements.

fn __setitem__(mut self, indices: List[Int], value: Scalar) raises
fn __setitem__(mut self, indices: List[Int], value: AnyTensor) raises

Example:

var x = zeros[DType.float32](3, 4)

# Set single element
x[List[Int](0, 0)] = 1.0

# Set row
x[List[Int](1)] = ones[DType.float32](4)

docs.title: slice
docs.text:
Extract a contiguous subtensor.

fn slice(self, starts: List[Int], ends: List[Int]) raises -> AnyTensor

Parameters:

  • starts: Start indices for each dimension
  • ends: End indices for each dimension (exclusive)

Example:

var x = randn[DType.float32](10, 20)

# Slice rows 2-5, columns 5-15
var starts = List[Int](2, 5)
var ends = List[Int](5, 15)
var y = x.slice(starts, ends)  # Shape: (3, 10)

docs.title: slice_along_axis
docs.text:
Slice along a single axis.

fn slice_along_axis(self, axis: Int, start: Int, end: Int) raises -> AnyTensor

Example:

var x = randn[DType.float32](32, 64, 128)

# Slice first 16 along axis 0
var y = x.slice_along_axis(axis=0, start=0, end=16)  # Shape: (16, 64, 128)

# Slice middle section along axis 2
var z = x.slice_along_axis(axis=2, start=32, end=96)  # Shape: (32, 64, 64)

docs.title: index_select
docs.text:
Select elements along an axis using indices tensor.

fn index_select(self, axis: Int, indices: AnyTensor) raises -> AnyTensor

Parameters:

  • axis: Axis to select along
  • indices: 1D tensor of indices to select

Example:

var x = randn[DType.float32](5, 3)

# Select rows 0, 2, 4
var idx = arange(0.0, 5.0, 2.0, DType.int32)  # [0, 2, 4]
var y = x.index_select(axis=0, idx)  # Shape: (3, 3)

docs.title: gather
docs.text:
Gather elements using multi-dimensional indices.

fn gather(self, axis: Int, indices: AnyTensor) raises -> AnyTensor

Parameters:

  • axis: Axis to gather along
  • indices: Index tensor (same shape as output)

Example:

var x = randn[DType.float32](3, 4)

# Gather specific elements
var indices = zeros[DType.int32](3, 2)  # 3x2 indices
var y = x.gather(axis=1, indices)  # Shape: (3, 2)

docs.title: scatter
docs.text:
Scatter values into tensor at specified indices.

fn scatter(mut self, axis: Int, indices: AnyTensor, values: AnyTensor) raises

Example:

var x = zeros[DType.float32](3, 4)
var indices = zeros[DType.int32](3, 2)
var values = ones[DType.float32](3, 2)
x.scatter(axis=1, indices, values)

docs.title: masked_select
docs.text:
Select elements where mask is true.

fn masked_select(self, mask: AnyTensor) raises -> AnyTensor

Parameters:

  • mask: Boolean tensor (same shape as self)

Returns: 1D tensor of selected elements

Example:

var x = randn[DType.float32](3, 4)
var mask = x > 0.0  # Boolean mask
var positive = x.masked_select(mask)  # 1D tensor

docs.title: masked_fill
docs.text:
Fill elements where mask is true.

fn masked_fill(mut self, mask: AnyTensor, value: Scalar) raises

Example:

var x = randn[DType.float32](3, 4)
var mask = x < 0.0
x.masked_fill(mask, 0.0)  # Set negative values to zero

docs.title: where
docs.text:
Select elements from two tensors based on condition.

fn where(condition: AnyTensor, x: AnyTensor, y: AnyTensor) raises -> AnyTensor

Example:

from odyssey.core import where, randn, zeros

var condition = randn[DType.float32](3, 4) > 0.0
var x = ones[DType.float32](3, 4)
var y = zeros[DType.float32](3, 4)
var result = where(condition, x, y)  # 1 where positive, 0 otherwise

docs.title: concatenate
docs.text:
Join tensors along an existing axis.

fn concatenate(tensors: List[AnyTensor], axis: Int = 0) raises -> AnyTensor

Example:

from odyssey.core import concatenate, randn

var a = randn[DType.float32](2, 3)
var b = randn[DType.float32](3, 3)
var c = concatenate(List[AnyTensor](a, b), axis=0)  # Shape: (5, 3)

docs.title: stack
docs.text:
Join tensors along a new axis.

fn stack(tensors: List[AnyTensor], axis: Int = 0) raises -> AnyTensor

Example:

from odyssey.core import stack, randn

var a = randn[DType.float32](3, 4)
var b = randn[DType.float32](3, 4)
var c = stack(List[AnyTensor](a, b), axis=0)  # Shape: (2, 3, 4)

docs.title: split
docs.text:
Split tensor into chunks.

fn split(self, chunks: Int, axis: Int = 0) raises -> List[AnyTensor]

Example:

var x = randn[DType.float32](10, 4)
var chunks = x.split(5, axis=0)  # 5 tensors of shape (2, 4)

docs.title: view
docs.text:
Create a view with different shape (no copy).

fn view(self, new_shape: List[Int]) raises -> AnyTensor

Example:

var x = randn[DType.float32](6, 4)
var y = x.view(List[Int](2, 3, 4))  # Shares data with x

docs.title: flatten
docs.text:
Flatten to 1D tensor.

fn flatten(self) raises -> AnyTensor

Example:

var x = randn[DType.float32](2, 3, 4)
var y = x.flatten()  # Shape: (24,)

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Shape Operations - reshape, squeeze, unsqueeze
  • Arithmetic Operations - Element-wise operations
  • AnyTensor Reference - Core tensor class

docs.title: Linear Algebra Operations
docs.text: Matrix operations and linear algebra routines.

docs.title: matmul (@)
docs.text:
Matrix multiplication following NumPy broadcasting rules.

fn __matmul__(self, other: AnyTensor) raises -> AnyTensor
fn matmul(a: AnyTensor, b: AnyTensor) raises -> AnyTensor

Parameters:

  • a: First tensor (at least 1D)
  • b: Second tensor (at least 1D)

Shape Rules:

  • (n,) @ (n,) -> () scalar (dot product)
  • (m, n) @ (n,) -> (m,) matrix-vector
  • (n,) @ (n, p) -> (p,) vector-matrix
  • (m, n) @ (n, p) -> (m, p) matrix-matrix
  • (b, m, n) @ (b, n, p) -> (b, m, p) batched

Examples:

from odyssey.core import randn, matmul

# Matrix-matrix multiplication
var A = randn[DType.float32](3, 4)
var B = randn[DType.float32](4, 5)
var C = A @ B  # Shape: (3, 5)
var D = matmul(A, B)  # Same as A @ B

# Matrix-vector multiplication
var x = randn[DType.float32](4)
var y = A @ x  # Shape: (3,)

# Batched matrix multiplication
var batch_A = randn[DType.float32](32, 64, 128)
var batch_B = randn[DType.float32](32, 128, 256)
var batch_C = batch_A @ batch_B  # Shape: (32, 64, 256)

docs.title: dot
docs.text:
Dot product of two 1D tensors.

fn dot(a: AnyTensor, b: AnyTensor) raises -> AnyTensor

Parameters:

  • a: 1D tensor
  • b: 1D tensor (same length as a)

Example:

from odyssey.core import randn, dot

var a = randn[DType.float32](100)
var b = randn[DType.float32](100)
var result = dot(a, b)  # Shape: () - scalar

docs.title: outer
docs.text:
Outer product of two 1D tensors.

fn outer(a: AnyTensor, b: AnyTensor) raises -> AnyTensor

Parameters:

  • a: 1D tensor of shape (m,)
  • b: 1D tensor of shape (n,)

Returns: 2D tensor of shape (m, n)

Example:

from odyssey.core import randn, outer

var a = randn[DType.float32](3)
var b = randn[DType.float32](4)
var result = outer(a, b)  # Shape: (3, 4)

docs.title: transpose / T
docs.text:
Transpose the tensor (swap last two dimensions).

fn transpose(self) raises -> AnyTensor
fn T(self) raises -> AnyTensor  # Property alias

Example:

var A = randn[DType.float32](3, 4)
var AT = A.T  # Shape: (4, 3)
var AT2 = A.transpose()  # Same as A.T

docs.title: permute
docs.text:
Permute dimensions in arbitrary order.

fn permute(self, dims: List[Int]) raises -> AnyTensor

Parameters:

  • dims: New order of dimensions

Example:

var x = randn[DType.float32](2, 3, 4, 5)
var y = x.permute(List[Int](0, 2, 1, 3))  # Shape: (2, 4, 3, 5)

docs.title: norm
docs.text:
Compute tensor norm.

fn norm(self, p: Float = 2.0, axis: Optional[Int] = None) raises -> AnyTensor

Parameters:

  • p: Norm order (1, 2, inf, etc.)
  • axis: Axis to compute norm along (None for all elements)

Example:

var x = randn[DType.float32](3, 4)

# Frobenius norm (L2 over all elements)
var frob = x.norm()

# L2 norm along axis 1
var row_norms = x.norm(p=2.0, axis=1)  # Shape: (3,)

# L1 norm
var l1 = x.norm(p=1.0)

# Infinity norm
var linf = x.norm(p=Float.inf)

docs.title: diag
docs.text:
Extract diagonal or create diagonal matrix.

fn diag(self, offset: Int = 0) raises -> AnyTensor

Parameters:

  • offset: Diagonal offset (0 = main, positive = above, negative = below)

Example:

from odyssey.core import randn, eye

# Extract diagonal from matrix
var A = randn[DType.float32](4, 4)
var d = A.diag()  # Shape: (4,) - main diagonal

# Create diagonal matrix from vector
var v = randn[DType.float32](3)
var D = v.diag()  # Shape: (3, 3)

docs.title: trace
docs.text:
Compute the trace (sum of diagonal elements).

fn trace(self) raises -> AnyTensor

Example:

var A = eye(4, DType.float32)  # 4x4 identity
var t = A.trace()  # 4.0

docs.title: svd
docs.text:
Singular value decomposition.

fn svd(self) raises -> Tuple[AnyTensor, AnyTensor, AnyTensor]

Returns: (U, S, Vh) where A = U @ diag(S) @ Vh

Example:

var A = randn[DType.float32](5, 3)
var (U, S, Vh) = A.svd()
# U: (5, 3), S: (3,), Vh: (3, 3)

docs.title: qr
docs.text:
QR decomposition.

fn qr(self) raises -> Tuple[AnyTensor, AnyTensor]

Returns: (Q, R) where A = Q @ R

Example:

var A = randn[DType.float32](5, 3)
var (Q, R) = A.qr()
# Q: (5, 3), R: (3, 3)

docs.title: solve
docs.text:
Solve linear system Ax = b.

fn solve(A: AnyTensor, b: AnyTensor) raises -> AnyTensor

Parameters:

  • A: Coefficient matrix (n, n)
  • b: Right-hand side vector (n,) or matrix (n, m)

Example:

from odyssey.core import randn, solve

var A = randn[DType.float32](3, 3)
var b = randn[DType.float32](3)
var x = solve(A, b)  # Ax = b

docs.title: inv
docs.text:
Compute matrix inverse.

fn inv(self) raises -> AnyTensor

Example:

var A = randn[DType.float32](3, 3)
var A_inv = A.inv()
# A @ A_inv ≈ eye(3)

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Arithmetic Operations - Element-wise operations
  • Reduction Operations - Sum, mean, max, min
  • AnyTensor Reference - Core tensor class

docs.title: Reduction Operations
docs.text: Operations that reduce tensor dimensions by aggregating values.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Reduction operations collapse one or more dimensions of a tensor by computing an aggregate value (sum, mean, max, etc.).

docs.title: Sum
docs.text:
Compute the sum of tensor elements.

fn sum(self, axis: Optional[Int] = None, keepdim: Bool = False) raises -> AnyTensor

Parameters:

  • axis: Dimension to reduce (None for all elements)
  • keepdim: Keep reduced dimension as size 1

Examples:

from odyssey.core import randn

var x = randn[DType.float32](3, 4)

# Sum all elements
var total = x.sum()  # Shape: () - scalar

# Sum along axis 0
var col_sums = x.sum(axis=0)  # Shape: (4,)

# Sum along axis 1, keep dimension
var row_sums = x.sum(axis=1, keepdim=True)  # Shape: (3, 1)

docs.title: Mean
docs.text:
Compute the mean of tensor elements.

fn mean(self, axis: Optional[Int] = None, keepdim: Bool = False) raises -> AnyTensor

Parameters:

  • axis: Dimension to reduce (None for all elements)
  • keepdim: Keep reduced dimension as size 1

Examples:

var x = randn[DType.float32](3, 4)

# Mean of all elements
var avg = x.mean()  # Shape: ()

# Mean along axis 0
var col_means = x.mean(axis=0)  # Shape: (4,)

# Mean along axis 1, keep dimension
var row_means = x.mean(axis=1, keepdim=True)  # Shape: (3, 1)

docs.title: Max
docs.text:
Compute the maximum value of tensor elements.

fn max(self, axis: Optional[Int] = None, keepdim: Bool = False) raises -> AnyTensor

Parameters:

  • axis: Dimension to reduce (None for all elements)
  • keepdim: Keep reduced dimension as size 1

Examples:

var x = randn[DType.float32](3, 4)

# Maximum of all elements
var maximum = x.max()  # Shape: ()

# Maximum along axis 0
var col_max = x.max(axis=0)  # Shape: (4,)

# Maximum along axis 1, keep dimension
var row_max = x.max(axis=1, keepdim=True)  # Shape: (3, 1)

docs.title: Min
docs.text:
Compute the minimum value of tensor elements.

fn min(self, axis: Optional[Int] = None, keepdim: Bool = False) raises -> AnyTensor

Parameters:

  • axis: Dimension to reduce (None for all elements)
  • keepdim: Keep reduced dimension as size 1

Examples:

var x = randn[DType.float32](3, 4)

# Minimum of all elements
var minimum = x.min()  # Shape: ()

# Minimum along axis 0
var col_min = x.min(axis=0)  # Shape: (4,)

# Minimum along axis 1, keep dimension
var row_min = x.min(axis=1, keepdim=True)  # Shape: (3, 1)

docs.title: Argmax
docs.text:
Find the index of the maximum value.

fn argmax(self, axis: Optional[Int] = None) raises -> AnyTensor

Parameters:

  • axis: Dimension to reduce (None for flattened index)

Examples:

var x = randn[DType.float32](3, 4)

# Index of maximum in flattened tensor
var idx = x.argmax()  # Shape: () - single index

# Indices of maximum along axis 0
var col_argmax = x.argmax(axis=0)  # Shape: (4,) - index per column

# Indices of maximum along axis 1
var row_argmax = x.argmax(axis=1)  # Shape: (3,) - index per row

docs.title: Argmin
docs.text:
Find the index of the minimum value.

fn argmin(self, axis: Optional[Int] = None) raises -> AnyTensor

Parameters:

  • axis: Dimension to reduce (None for flattened index)

Examples:

var x = randn[DType.float32](3, 4)

# Index of minimum in flattened tensor
var idx = x.argmin()  # Shape: ()

# Indices of minimum along axis 1
var row_argmin = x.argmin(axis=1)  # Shape: (3,)

docs.title: Prod
docs.text:
Compute the product of tensor elements.

fn prod(self, axis: Optional[Int] = None, keepdim: Bool = False) raises -> AnyTensor

Parameters:

  • axis: Dimension to reduce (None for all elements)
  • keepdim: Keep reduced dimension as size 1

Examples:

var x = full(List[Int](2, 3), 2.0, DType.float32)  # All 2s
var product = x.prod()  # 64.0 (2^6)

docs.title: Any / All
docs.text:
Logical reduction operations.

fn any(self, axis: Optional[Int] = None) raises -> AnyTensor
fn all(self, axis: Optional[Int] = None) raises -> AnyTensor

Examples:

var x = zeros[DType.float32](3, 4)
x[List[Int](0, 0)] = 1.0  # Set one element to non-zero

var has_nonzero = x.any()  # True
var all_nonzero = x.all()  # False

docs.title: Keepdim Behavior
docs.text:
The keepdim parameter controls output shape:

Input shape: (3, 4, 5)

# Without keepdim (default)
sum(axis=1).shape()  -> (3, 5)     # Dimension removed

# With keepdim=True
sum(axis=1, keepdim=True).shape()  -> (3, 1, 5)  # Dimension kept as 1

keepdim=True is useful for broadcasting the result back to the original shape.

docs.title: Negative Axis
docs.text:
Negative axis values count from the end:

var x = randn[DType.float32](3, 4, 5)

x.sum(axis=-1)  # Same as axis=2, shape: (3, 4)
x.sum(axis=-2)  # Same as axis=1, shape: (3, 5)

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Arithmetic Operations - Element-wise operations
  • Linear Algebra - Matrix operations
  • AnyTensor Reference - Core tensor class

docs.title: Data Loading
docs.text: Dataset and DataLoader utilities for training.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
ML Odyssey provides data loading utilities for efficient batch processing:

from odyssey.training.data import Dataset, DataLoader

docs.title: Dataset
docs.text: Base class for datasets.

docs.title: Creating a Dataset
docs.text:
struct MyDataset(Dataset):
    var data: AnyTensor
    var labels: AnyTensor

    fn __init__(out self, data: AnyTensor, labels: AnyTensor):
        self.data = data
        self.labels = labels

    fn __len__(self) -> Int:
        return self.data.shape()[0]

    fn __getitem__(self, index: Int) raises -> Tuple[AnyTensor, AnyTensor]:
        return (self.data[index], self.labels[index])

from odyssey.training.data import MNIST

var train_dataset = MNIST(root="./data", train=True, download=True)

print("Training samples:", len(train_dataset))  # 60000

docs.title: DataLoader
docs.text: Batches and shuffles data for training.

docs.title: Constructor
docs.text:
fn __init__(
    out self,
    dataset: Dataset,
    batch_size: Int = 1,
    shuffle: Bool = False,
    drop_last: Bool = False,
) raises

Parameters:

  • dataset: Dataset to load from
  • batch_size: Samples per batch (default: 1)
  • shuffle: Randomize order each epoch (default: False)
  • drop_last: Drop incomplete final batch (default: False)

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
from odyssey.training.data import DataLoader, MNIST

var dataset = MNIST(root="./data", train=True)
var dataloader = DataLoader(
    dataset,
    batch_size=32,
    shuffle=True,
    drop_last=True,
)

# Iterate over batches
for batch in dataloader:
    var images = batch.data      # Shape: (32, 1, 28, 28)
    var labels = batch.labels    # Shape: (32,)
    # ... training step ...

docs.title: Batch Structure
docs.text:
Each batch contains:

struct Batch:
    var data: AnyTensor    # Input features
    var labels: AnyTensor  # Target labels

docs.title: Data Transforms
docs.text: Apply transformations to data.

docs.title: Normalize
docs.text:
Normalize tensor with mean and std.

from odyssey.training.data.transforms import Normalize

var normalize = Normalize(mean=[0.1307], std=[0.3081])  # MNIST stats
var normalized = normalize(image)

docs.title: ToTensor
docs.text:
Convert to tensor and scale to [0, 1].

from odyssey.training.data.transforms import ToTensor

var to_tensor = ToTensor()
var tensor = to_tensor(raw_data)

docs.title: Compose
docs.text:
Chain multiple transforms.

from odyssey.training.data.transforms import Compose, ToTensor, Normalize

var transform = Compose([
    ToTensor(),

var dataset = MNIST(root="./data", transform=transform)

docs.title: Data Augmentation

    RandomHorizontalFlip,

)

var train_transform = Compose([

docs.title: Train/Validation Split
docs.text:
Split dataset for training and validation.

from odyssey.training.data import random_split

var dataset = MNIST(root="./data", train=True)

# 80% train, 20% validation
var train_size = Int(0.8 * len(dataset))
var val_size = len(dataset) - train_size
var (train_dataset, val_dataset) = random_split(
    dataset,
    [train_size, val_size],
)

var train_loader = DataLoader(train_dataset, batch_size=32, shuffle=True)
var val_loader = DataLoader(val_dataset, batch_size=32, shuffle=False)

docs.title: From NumPy Arrays
docs.text:
from odyssey.training.data import TensorDataset

var X = randn[DType.float32](1000, 784)  # Features
var y = zeros[DType.int32](1000)         # Labels

var dataset = TensorDataset(X, y)
var loader = DataLoader(dataset, batch_size=32)

docs.title: From Files
docs.text:
struct ImageFolderDataset(Dataset):
    var image_paths: List[String]
    var labels: List[Int]

    fn __init__(out self, root: String) raises:
        # Load image paths and labels from directory structure
        # root/class1/img1.png, root/class2/img2.png, ...
        ...

    fn __len__(self) -> Int:
        return len(self.image_paths)

    fn __getitem__(self, index: Int) raises -> Tuple[AnyTensor, AnyTensor]:
        var image = load_image(self.image_paths[index])
        var label = self.labels[index]
        return (image, label)

docs.title: Complete Training Example
docs.text:
from odyssey.training.data import MNIST, DataLoader
from odyssey.training.data.transforms import Compose, ToTensor, Normalize
from odyssey.training.optimizers import Adam
from odyssey.core.layers import Linear, ReLU, Sequential

# Prepare data
var transform = Compose([
    ToTensor(),
    Normalize(mean=[0.1307], std=[0.3081]),
])

var train_dataset = MNIST(root="./data", train=True, transform=transform)
var test_dataset = MNIST(root="./data", train=False, transform=transform)

var train_loader = DataLoader(train_dataset, batch_size=64, shuffle=True)
var test_loader = DataLoader(test_dataset, batch_size=64, shuffle=False)

# Create model
var model = Sequential()
model.add(Flatten())
model.add(Linear(784, 128))
model.add(ReLU())
model.add(Linear(128, 10))

var optimizer = Adam(model.parameters(), lr=0.001)
var criterion = CrossEntropyLoss()

# Training loop
for epoch in range(10):
    model.train()
    for batch in train_loader:
        var tape = Tape()
        with tape:
            var output = model.forward(batch.data)
            var loss = criterion.forward(output, batch.labels)

        optimizer.zero_grad()
        tape.backward(loss)
        optimizer.step()

    # Validation
    model.set_inference_mode()
    var correct = 0
    var total = 0
    with no_grad():
        for batch in test_loader:
            var output = model.forward(batch.data)
            var predictions = output.argmax(dim=-1)
            correct += (predictions == batch.labels).sum().item[DType.int32]()
            total += batch.labels.numel()

    print("Epoch", epoch, "Accuracy:", correct / total)

docs.title: Performance Tips
docs.text:
1. Use appropriate batch size - Larger batches for GPU, smaller for CPU
  2. Enable shuffle for training - Improves convergence
  3. Use drop_last=True - Avoids small final batches
  4. Prefetch data - Load next batch while computing

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Optimizers - Training optimization
  • Autograd - Gradient computation
  • AnyTensor Reference - Core tensor class

docs.title: Optimizers
docs.text: Optimization algorithms for training neural networks.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Optimizers update model parameters based on computed gradients:

from odyssey.training.optimizers import SGD, Adam, AdamW

All optimizers share a common interface:

trait Optimizer:
    fn step(mut self) raises -> None
    fn zero_grad(mut self) raises -> None

docs.title: SGD
docs.text:
Stochastic Gradient Descent with optional momentum.

from odyssey.training.optimizers import SGD

Constructor:

fn __init__(
    out self,
    parameters: List[AnyTensor],
    lr: Float,
    momentum: Float = 0.0,
    weight_decay: Float = 0.0,
    nesterov: Bool = False,
) raises

Parameters:

  • parameters: Model parameters to optimize
  • lr: Learning rate
  • momentum: Momentum factor (default: 0.0)
  • weight_decay: L2 regularization (default: 0.0)
  • nesterov: Use Nesterov momentum (default: False)

Example:

from odyssey.training.optimizers import SGD

var model = create_model()
var optimizer = SGD(
    model.parameters(),
    lr=0.01,
    momentum=0.9,
    weight_decay=1e-4,
)

# Training loop
for epoch in range(num_epochs):
    for batch in dataloader:
        optimizer.zero_grad()
        var output = model.forward(batch.input)
        var loss = criterion.forward(output, batch.target)
        var grads = tape.backward(loss)
        optimizer.step()

docs.title: Adam
docs.text:
Adaptive Moment Estimation optimizer.

from odyssey.training.optimizers import Adam

Constructor:

fn __init__(
    out self,
    parameters: List[AnyTensor],
    lr: Float = 0.001,
    betas: Tuple[Float, Float] = (0.9, 0.999),
    eps: Float = 1e-8,
    weight_decay: Float = 0.0,
) raises

Parameters:

  • parameters: Model parameters to optimize
  • lr: Learning rate (default: 0.001)
  • betas: Coefficients for running averages (default: (0.9, 0.999))
  • eps: Numerical stability term (default: 1e-8)
  • weight_decay: L2 regularization (default: 0.0)

Example:

from odyssey.training.optimizers import Adam

var optimizer = Adam(
    model.parameters(),
    lr=0.001,
    betas=(0.9, 0.999),
)

Properties:

  • Adaptive learning rates per parameter
  • Works well with default hyperparameters
  • Good for sparse gradients

docs.title: AdamW
docs.text:
Adam with decoupled weight decay (recommended over Adam).

from odyssey.training.optimizers import AdamW

Constructor:

fn __init__(
    out self,
    parameters: List[AnyTensor],
    lr: Float = 0.001,
    betas: Tuple[Float, Float] = (0.9, 0.999),
    eps: Float = 1e-8,
    weight_decay: Float = 0.01,
) raises

Example:

from odyssey.training.optimizers import AdamW

var optimizer = AdamW(
    model.parameters(),
    lr=0.001,
    weight_decay=0.01,
)

Difference from Adam:

  • Weight decay applied directly to weights, not gradients
  • Better generalization, especially with large models

docs.title: RMSprop
docs.text:
Root Mean Square Propagation.

from odyssey.training.optimizers import RMSprop

Constructor:

fn __init__(
    out self,
    parameters: List[AnyTensor],
    lr: Float = 0.01,
    alpha: Float = 0.99,
    eps: Float = 1e-8,
    momentum: Float = 0.0,
    weight_decay: Float = 0.0,
) raises

Example:

from odyssey.training.optimizers import RMSprop

var optimizer = RMSprop(
    model.parameters(),
    lr=0.01,
    alpha=0.99,
)

docs.title: LARS
docs.text:
Layer-wise Adaptive Rate Scaling (for large batch training).

from odyssey.training.optimizers import LARS

Constructor:

fn __init__(
    out self,
    parameters: List[AnyTensor],
    lr: Float,
    momentum: Float = 0.9,
    weight_decay: Float = 0.0,
    trust_coefficient: Float = 0.001,
) raises

Example:

from odyssey.training.optimizers import LARS

# For large batch sizes (e.g., 4096+)
var optimizer = LARS(
    model.parameters(),
    lr=0.1,
    momentum=0.9,
    trust_coefficient=0.001,
)

docs.title: step
docs.text:
Apply gradients to parameters.

optimizer.step()

docs.title: zero_grad
docs.text:
Reset gradients to zero before next backward pass.

optimizer.zero_grad()

Important: Always call zero_grad() before computing new gradients.

docs.title: state_dict / load_state_dict
docs.text:
Save and restore optimizer state.

# Save state
var state = optimizer.state_dict()

# Load state
optimizer.load_state_dict(state)

docs.title: Learning Rate Scheduling
docs.text: Adjust learning rate during training.

docs.title: StepLR
docs.text:
Decay LR by factor every N steps.

from odyssey.training.schedulers import StepLR

var scheduler = StepLR(optimizer, step_size=30, gamma=0.1)

for epoch in range(100):
    train_epoch()
    scheduler.step()  # LR *= 0.1 every 30 epochs

docs.title: CosineAnnealingLR
docs.text:
Cosine annealing schedule.

from odyssey.training.schedulers import CosineAnnealingLR

var scheduler = CosineAnnealingLR(optimizer, T_max=100)

docs.title: OneCycleLR
docs.text:
One-cycle learning rate policy.

from odyssey.training.schedulers import OneCycleLR

var scheduler = OneCycleLR(
    optimizer,
    max_lr=0.1,
    total_steps=1000,
)

for step in range(1000):
    train_step()
    scheduler.step()

docs.title: Gradient Clipping
docs.text:
Prevent exploding gradients.

from odyssey.training import clip_grad_norm, clip_grad_value

# Clip by global L2 norm
clip_grad_norm(model.parameters(), max_norm=1.0)

# Clip by value
clip_grad_value(model.parameters(), clip_value=0.5)

docs.title: Complete Training Loop
docs.text:
from odyssey.training.optimizers import AdamW
from odyssey.training.schedulers import CosineAnnealingLR
from odyssey.training import clip_grad_norm
from odyssey.autograd import Tape

var model = create_model()
var optimizer = AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=0.001)
var scheduler = CosineAnnealingLR(optimizer, T_max=100)
var criterion = CrossEntropyLoss()

for epoch in range(100):
    model.train()
    for batch in train_loader:
        # Forward pass
        var tape = Tape()
        with tape:
            var output = model.forward(batch.input)
            var loss = criterion.forward(output, batch.target)

        # Backward pass
        optimizer.zero_grad()
        var grads = tape.backward(loss)

        # Gradient clipping
        clip_grad_norm(model.parameters(), max_norm=1.0)

        # Update weights
        optimizer.step()

    # Update learning rate
    scheduler.step()

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
This document tracks the progress of updating all 30 agent files with Claude 4-specific sections following official best practices from:

  • https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/build-with-claude/prompt-engineering/claude-4-best-practices
  • https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agents-and-tools/agent-skills/best-practices
  • https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents

docs.title: Required Sections
docs.text:
Each agent file must include:

  1. Thinking Guidance - When to use extended thinking vs standard
  2. Output Preferences - Format, style, code examples, decisions
  3. Delegation Patterns - When to use skills vs sub-agents
  4. Sub-Agent Usage - How to spawn sub-agents with proper context

docs.title: Automation Script
docs.text:
A Python script has been created to automate the updates:

Location: /scripts/update_agents_claude4.py

Features:

  • Categorizes agents by level and role
  • Generates appropriate thinking guidance for each category
  • Customizes output preferences and delegation patterns
  • Inserts sections before existing references

  • Skips files already updated

  • Provides progress reporting

Usage:

bash python3 scripts/update_agents_claude4.pytext

The script handles:

  • Different agent categories (chief-architect, orchestrator, design, specialist, engineer, junior)
  • Role-specific thinking tasks and budgets
  • Appropriate skill and sub-agent recommendations
  • Context examples tailored to agent level

docs.title: Thinking Guidance Template
docs.text:
Level 0-1 (Strategic):

  • Extended thinking for: Architecture decisions, cross-section conflicts, paper analysis
  • Standard thinking for: Routine delegation, status updates

Level 2-3 (Tactical):

  • Extended thinking for: Complex design trade-offs, algorithm selection, performance optimization
  • Standard thinking for: Standard component work, routine reviews

Level 4-5 (Execution):

  • Extended thinking for: Complex implementations, debugging subtle issues, SIMD optimization
  • Standard thinking for: Standard functions, routine maintenance

docs.title: Output Preferences Template
docs.text:
All Levels:

  • Format: Structured Markdown
  • Style: Varies by level (strategic → architectural → technical → implementation-focused)
  • Code examples: More detailed at lower levels, minimal at higher levels
  • Decisions: Clear rationale sections appropriate to level

docs.title: Delegation Patterns Template
docs.text:
Skills:

  • Level-appropriate skill recommendations
  • Clear "when to invoke" guidance

Sub-agents:

  • When appropriate (not for Level 5, rare for Level 4)
  • Clear "Do NOT use for" guidance
  • Escalation patterns

docs.title: Sub-Agent Usage Template (when applicable)
docs.text:
Context to provide:

  • File paths with line numbers
  • Issue numbers
  • Clear success criteria
  • Scope boundaries

Example invocations:

  • Tailored to agent's domain
  • Specific deliverables
  • Measurable success criteria

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
1. Run automation script:

bash cd python3 scripts/update_agents_claude4.pytext

  1. Review generated content:
  2. Check a sample from each category
  3. Verify insertion points are correct

  4. Ensure examples are appropriate

  5. Manual adjustments:

  6. Review specialists may need lighter sub-agent guidance
  7. Junior engineers need simpler language

  8. Domain-specific customizations

  9. Update templates:

  10. Update agent templates in agents/templates/ with new sections

  11. Ensure future agents include Claude 4 sections from the start

  12. Validation:

  13. Run python3 tests/agents/validate_configs.py .claude/agents/
  14. Verify no broken markdown links

  15. Check for consistent formatting

  16. Commit and PR:

```bash git add .claude/agents/*.md docs/dev/agent-claude4-update-status.md scripts/update_agents_claude4.py git commit -m "docs(agents): update agent files for Claude 4 best practices" git push gh pr create --title "docs(agents): Update all agent files for Claude 4 best practices" \

 --body "Closes #2548" \
 --label "documentation"

```text
