docs.title: Papers and Shared Library Integration Guide
docs.text: This guide explains how papers/ and src/odyssey/ directories work together.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
• papers/: Individual paper implementations
  • src/odyssey/: Reusable ML/AI components

docs.title: Integration Pattern
docs.text:
Papers import from shared:

mojomojo

from odyssey.core import Layer, Module from odyssey.training import Optimizer from odyssey.data import Dataset

```text

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text: See quick-start-new-paper.md for creating new papers.

docs.title: Quick Start: Creating a New Paper Implementation
Steps
docs.text:
1. Copy template: cp -r papers/_template papers/my-paper
  2. Update README with paper details
  3. Implement model in src/model.mojo
  4. Use shared components from src/odyssey/
  5. Run tests: mojo test papers/my-paper/tests/

docs.title: Example
docs.text: See existing papers for examples of shared library usage.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The five supporting directories (benchmarks/, docs/, agents/, tools/, configs/) provide critical infrastructure for ML Odyssey. This guide explains how they work together, common integration patterns, and best practices for using them in combination.

docs.title: Quick Reference
docs.text: Directory Purpose Primary Users Key Integration Points benchmarks/ Performance measurement Developers, CI/CD papers/, src/odyssey/, configs/ docs/ User documentation All users All directories agents/ AI automation Claude Code All directories tools/ Development utilities Developers papers/, configs/, tests/ configs/ Configuration management All users papers/, benchmarks/, src/odyssey/

docs.title: Pattern 1: New Paper Implementation
docs.text:
Complete workflow using all supporting directories

bashbash

docs.title: Step 1: Use tools/ to scaffold paper structure
docs.text: python tools/paper-scaffold/scaffold.py \ --paper resnet \ --title "Deep Residual Learning" \ --authors "He et al." \ --year 2015

docs.title: Step 2: Create configs/ for the paper
docs.text: cp configs/templates/paper.yaml configs/papers/resnet/model.yaml cp configs/templates/experiment.yaml configs/experiments/resnet/baseline.yaml

docs.title: Step 4: Add benchmarks/ for performance tracking
... update docs/api/papers/resnet.md
docs.text:
```text

Directory Interactions:

  1. tools/ → papers/ (scaffolding)
  2. configs/templates/ → configs/papers/ (configuration)
  3. papers/ + configs/ → Training
  4. benchmarks/ ← papers/ (performance measurement)
  5. docs/ ← All directories (documentation)

docs.title: Pattern 2: Performance Optimization
docs.text:
Using benchmarks/ and tools/ together

```bash

```bash

docs.title: Step 1: Run benchmarks/ to identify bottleneck
docs.text: mojo benchmarks/scripts/run_benchmarks.mojo --paper lenet5

docs.title: Step 2: Use tools/benchmarking/ for detailed profiling
docs.text: mojo tools/benchmarking/runner.mojo --target lenet5 --profile

docs.title: ... optimize code based on profiling results
Step 4: Re-run benchmarks/ to verify improvement
docs.text: mojo benchmarks/scripts/run_benchmarks.mojo --paper lenet5 --compare

docs.title: ... document optimization technique
docs.text: ```text

docs.title: Directory Interactions
docs.text:
1. benchmarks/ → Identify bottleneck
  2. tools/benchmarking/ → Profile specific code
  3. papers/ → Implement optimization
  4. benchmarks/ → Verify improvement
  5. docs/advanced/ → Document technique

docs.title: Using configs/ across all directories
docs.text: bashbash

docs.title: Step 1: Create experiment config
docs.text: cp configs/templates/experiment.yaml configs/experiments/lenet5/augmented.yaml

docs.title: Step 3: Run training with config
docs.text: mojo papers/lenet5/train.mojo --config experiments/lenet5/augmented

docs.title: Step 4: Benchmark the experiment
docs.text: mojo benchmarks/scripts/run_benchmarks.mojo --experiment lenet5/augmented

docs.title: docs/research/experiments/lenet5_augmentation.md
docs.text:
```text

Directory Interactions:

  1. configs/templates/ → configs/experiments/ (creation)
  2. configs/experiments/ → papers/ (training)
  3. configs/experiments/ → benchmarks/ (performance)
  4. Results → docs/research/ (documentation)

docs.title: Pattern 4: Documentation Creation
docs.text:
Using agents/ to automate documentation

```bash

```bash

docs.title: tools/codegen/ → creates API docs from code
Step 4: Validate documentation
docs.text: python scripts/validate_links.py docs/

docs.title: Update docs/index.md with new links
docs.text: ```text

docs.title: Directory Interactions
docs.text:
1. agents/ → Analyze code structure
  2. tools/codegen/ → Generate API docs
  3. docs/ → Create and organize documentation
  4. scripts/ → Validate documentation quality

docs.title: All directories working with CI/CD
docs.text: yamlyaml

docs.title: .github/workflows/paper-validation.yml
docs.text:
name: Paper Validation

on: [push, pull_request]

jobs: test: runs-on: ubuntu-latest steps: # Use tools/ for setup

  - name: Setup environment
  - name: Setup environment

    run: python scripts/setup.py

  # Use configs/ for test configuration

  - name: Load test config
  - name: Load test config

    run: cp configs/defaults/testing.yaml .test_config.yaml

  # Run tests

  - name: Run tests
  - name: Run tests

    run: mojo test tests/papers/lenet5/

  # Use benchmarks/ for performance check

  - name: Run benchmarks
  - name: Run benchmarks

    run: mojo benchmarks/scripts/run_benchmarks.mojo

  # Use agents/ for code review

  - name: AI code review
  - name: AI code review

    run: python scripts/agents/review_pr.py

  # Validate docs/

  - name: Check documentation
  - name: Check documentation

    run: python scripts/validate_links.py docs/

```text

Directory Interactions:

  1. scripts/ → CI/CD setup
  2. configs/ → Test configuration
  3. tests/ → Test execution
  4. benchmarks/ → Performance verification
  5. agents/ → Automated code review
  6. docs/ → Documentation validation

docs.title: Dependency Map
docs.text:
```text

```text

papers/ ├─> src/odyssey/ (uses layers, optimizers) ├─> configs/ (loads configurations) └─> tools/ (uses utilities)

src/odyssey/ └─> configs/ (config loading utilities)

benchmarks/ ├─> papers/ (benchmarks implementations) ├─> src/odyssey/ (benchmarks components) └─> configs/ (benchmark configurations)

tools/ ├─> papers/ (scaffolds papers) ├─> configs/ (generates configs) └─> tests/ (generates test templates)

configs/ (no dependencies - foundation layer)

docs/ (documents all directories)

agents/ (coordinates all directories)

```text

docs.title: Foundation Layer
docs.text:
configs/ is the foundation - no dependencies:

  • Provides configuration to all other directories
  • Used by papers/, benchmarks/, tools/
  • Loaded by src/odyssey/utils/config_loader

docs.title: Integration Layer
docs.text:
tools/ integrates multiple directories:

  • Scaffolds papers/
  • Generates configs/
  • Creates test templates
  • Provides utilities for all

docs.title: Coordination Layer
docs.text:
agents/ coordinates all workflows:

  • Automates paper implementation
  • Manages code review
  • Generates documentation
  • Orchestrates multi-step workflows

docs.title: Scenario 1: Starting a New Paper
docs.text: Goal: Implement a new research paper efficiently

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Scaffold with tools/paper-scaffold/
  2. Configure with configs/templates/
  3. Implement in papers/{name}/
  4. Test with tests/papers/{name}/
  5. Benchmark with benchmarks/scripts/
  6. Document in docs/

docs.title: Integration
docs.text: • Tools generate structure → Papers implement → Configs provide parameters → Benchmarks measure → Docs explain

docs.title: Scenario 2: Optimizing Performance
docs.text: Goal: Improve performance of existing implementation

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Measure with benchmarks/scripts/
  2. Profile with tools/benchmarking/
  3. Optimize in papers/{name}/ or src/odyssey/
  4. Verify with benchmarks/scripts/
  5. Document in docs/advanced/

docs.title: Integration
docs.text: • Benchmarks identify issue → Tools profile → Code optimization → Benchmarks verify → Docs preserve knowledge

docs.title: Scenario 3: Running Experiments
docs.text: Goal: Test variations of a paper implementation

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Create experiment config in configs/experiments/
  2. Run training with papers/{name}/train.mojo
  3. Benchmark with benchmarks/scripts/
  4. Compare results across experiments
  5. Document findings in docs/research/

docs.title: Integration
docs.text: • Configs define variations → Papers execute → Benchmarks measure → Docs record results

docs.title: Scenario 4: Contributing Documentation
docs.text: Goal: Add or improve documentation

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Identify gap in docs/
  2. Write content following markdown standards
  3. Link from docs/index.md
  4. Validate with scripts/validate_links.py
  5. Review with agents/ (optional)

docs.title: Integration
docs.text: • Docs created → Scripts validate → Agents review → Team benefits

docs.title: Scenario 5: Automating Workflows
docs.text: Goal: Use agents to automate repetitive tasks

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Define agent in .claude/agents/
  2. Document in agents/
  3. Integrate with tools/ or scripts/
  4. Configure behavior with configs/
  5. Document usage in docs/

docs.title: Integration
docs.text: • Agents automate → Tools provide capabilities → Configs control behavior → Docs explain usage

docs.title: Integration Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Use Configs for All Parameters

Don't hardcode:

mojomojo

docs.title: Bad
docs.text: var learning_rate = 0.001 var batch_size = 32

docs.title: Good
docs.text:
var config = load_experiment_config("lenet5", "baseline") var learning_rate = config.get_float("optimizer.learning_rate") var batch_size = config.get_int("training.batch_size")

```text

2. Benchmark After Changes

Always run benchmarks after code changes:

```bash

```bash

docs.title: After implementing optimization
docs.text:
mojo benchmarks/scripts/run_benchmarks.mojo --compare

```text

3. Generate Before Implementing

Use tools to avoid boilerplate:

bashbash

docs.title: Generate structure first
docs.text: python tools/paper-scaffold/scaffold.py --paper new_paper

docs.title: Then implement
docs.text: cd papers/new_paper/

docs.title: ... implementation
docs.text:
```text

4. Document as You Go

Update documentation with code:

```bash

```bash

docs.title: After implementing feature
docs.text:
vim papers/new_paper/README.md # Update paper docs vim docs/api/papers/new_paper.md # Update API docs

```text

5. Validate Continuously

Run validation throughout development:

bashbash

docs.title: Validate structure
docs.text: python scripts/validate_structure.py

docs.title: Validate docs
docs.text: python scripts/validate_links.py

docs.title: Run tests
docs.text:
mojo test tests/

```text

docs.title: Anti-Patterns to Avoid
docs.text:
Don't: Bypass Configs

```mojo

```mojo

docs.title: Bad - hardcoded parameters
docs.text:
var lr = 0.001

```text

docs.title: Do: Use Config System
docs.text: mojomojo

docs.title: Good - configurable parameters
docs.text:
var config = load_paper_config("lenet5", "training") var lr = config.get_float("optimizer.learning_rate")

```text

Don't: Skip Benchmarking

```bash

```bash

docs.title: Bad - deploy without measuring
docs.text:
git commit -m "optimized code" && git push

```text

docs.title: Do: Benchmark Before Committing
docs.text: bashbash

docs.title: Good - verify performance
docs.text:
mojo benchmarks/scripts/run_benchmarks.mojo git commit -m "optimized code (15% faster)" && git push

```text

Don't: Duplicate Boilerplate

```bash

```bash

docs.title: Bad - manually create every file
docs.text: mkdir papers/new_paper touch papers/new_paper/model.mojo

docs.title: ... create 10 more files manually
docs.text: ```text

docs.title: Do: Use Scaffolding Tools
docs.text: bashbash

docs.title: Good - generate structure
docs.text:
python tools/paper-scaffold/scaffold.py --paper new_paper

```text

Don't: Document in Isolation

```text

```text

docs.title: Bad - docs not linked or indexed
docs.text:
papers/my_paper/some_notes.txt

```text

docs.title: Do: Integrate with Doc System
docs.text: texttext

docs.title: Good - proper location and linking
docs.text:
docs/research/my_paper_analysis.md docs/index.md (with link to analysis)

```text

docs.title: Issue: Config Not Loading
docs.text:
Symptoms: Configuration values not found or errors loading config

Check:

  1. File exists at expected path
  2. YAML syntax is valid
  3. Environment variables are set
  4. Config hierarchy is correct

Solution:

```bash

```bash

docs.title: Validate config syntax
docs.text: python scripts/lint_configs.py configs/experiments/my_experiment.yaml

docs.title: Check environment
docs.text: echo $ML_ODYSSEY_DATA

docs.title: Test loading
docs.text:
mojo -c "from odyssey.utils.config_loader import load_experiment_config; var c = load_experiment_config('paper', 'exp')"

```text

docs.title: Issue: Benchmark Regression
docs.text: Symptoms: Benchmarks show performance degradation

docs.title: Check
docs.text:
1. Recent code changes
  2. Configuration changes
  3. Dependency updates
  4. System resource contention

docs.title: Solution
docs.text: bashbash

docs.title: Compare with baseline
docs.text: mojo benchmarks/scripts/compare_results.mojo \ --baseline benchmarks/baselines/baseline_results.json \ --current benchmarks/results/latest_results.json

docs.title: Profile to find bottleneck
docs.text: mojo tools/benchmarking/runner.mojo --profile --target problem_area

docs.title: Review recent changes
docs.text:
git diff HEAD~1 papers/affected_paper/

```python

docs.title: Issue: Tool Not Found
docs.text:
Symptoms: Tool script not executing or import errors

Check:

  1. Tool exists in correct directory
  2. Python path includes tools/
  3. Dependencies installed
  4. File permissions correct

Solution:

```bash

```bash

docs.title: Check tool exists
docs.text: ls -la tools/paper-scaffold/scaffold.py

docs.title: Check permissions
docs.text: chmod +x tools/paper-scaffold/scaffold.py

docs.title: Add to Python path
docs.text: export PYTHONPATH="${PYTHONPATH}:/home/user/Odyssey/tools"

docs.title: Run with explicit path
docs.text:
python /home/user/Odyssey/tools/paper-scaffold/scaffold.py

```text

docs.title: Issue: Documentation Links Broken
docs.text: Symptoms: Link validation errors or 404s in docs

docs.title: Check
docs.text:
1. File paths are correct
  2. Relative paths used properly
  3. Files not moved or renamed
  4. Markdown syntax correct

docs.title: Solution
docs.text: bashbash

docs.title: Validate all links
docs.text: python scripts/validate_links.py docs/

docs.title: Check specific file
docs.text: python scripts/validate_links.py docs/path/to/file.md

docs.title: Fix and re-validate
docs.text:
vim docs/path/to/file.md # Fix links python scripts/validate_links.py docs/path/to/file.md

```text

docs.title: Multi-Directory Workflows
docs.text:
Complex Workflow Example: Paper + Optimization + Documentation

```bash

```bash

docs.title: Complete workflow using all 5 supporting directories
docs.text: PAPER="resnet" EXPERIMENT="baseline"

docs.title: 1. Tools: Scaffold paper structure
docs.text: echo "Step 1: Scaffolding paper structure..." python tools/paper-scaffold/scaffold.py \ --paper $PAPER \ --title "Deep Residual Learning" \ --authors "He et al." \ --year 2015

docs.title: 2. Configs: Create experiment configuration
docs.text: echo "Step 2: Creating experiment config..." cp configs/templates/experiment.yaml configs/experiments/$PAPER/$EXPERIMENT.yaml

docs.title: 3. Papers: Implement (manual step)
docs.text: echo "Step 3: Implement the paper in papers/$PAPER/" echo " - Edit papers/$PAPER/model.mojo" echo " - Edit papers/$PAPER/train.mojo"

docs.title: 4. Benchmarks: Establish baseline
docs.text: echo "Step 4: Running initial benchmarks..." mojo benchmarks/scripts/run_benchmarks.mojo --paper $PAPER > benchmarks/baselines/${PAPER}_baseline.json

docs.title: 5. Tools: Profile for optimization
docs.text: echo "Step 5: Profiling for optimization..." mojo tools/benchmarking/runner.mojo --target $PAPER --profile

docs.title: 6. Papers: Optimize based on profiling
docs.text: echo "Step 6: Implement optimizations..."

docs.title: 7. Benchmarks: Verify improvement
docs.text: echo "Step 7: Verifying performance improvement..." mojo benchmarks/scripts/run_benchmarks.mojo --paper $PAPER --compare

docs.title: 8. Docs: Document everything
docs.text: echo "Step 8: Creating documentation..."

docs.title: Create paper documentation
docs.text: vim docs/api/papers/$PAPER.md

docs.title: Create optimization guide
docs.text: vim docs/advanced/optimization_${PAPER}.md

docs.title: 9. Validate all
docs.text:
echo "Step 9: Running validation..." python scripts/validate_structure.py python scripts/validate_links.py mojo test tests/papers/$PAPER/

echo "Workflow complete!"

```text

docs.title: Using agents/ to orchestrate workflows
docs.text: yamlyaml

docs.title: PyTorch to ML Odyssey Migration Guide
docs.text: A comprehensive guide for migrating PyTorch models and workflows to ML Odyssey.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: ML Odyssey provides a familiar API for PyTorch users while leveraging Mojo's performance benefits. This guide covers the key differences and provides practical migration patterns.

docs.title: Quick Reference
docs.text: Concept PyTorch ML Odyssey Tensor torch.Tensor AnyTensor Module nn.Module Module trait Autograd Automatic Tape context Device .to('cuda') CPU (GPU coming) DType Inferred Explicit

docs.title: Creating Tensors
docs.text:
PyTorch:

import torch

# From list
x = torch.tensor([1, 2, 3])

# Zeros/ones
y = torch.zeros(10, 10)
z = torch.ones(5, 5)

# Random
r = torch.randn(3, 4)
u = torch.rand(3, 4)

# Range
a = torch.arange(0, 10, 1)

# Identity
I = torch.eye(3)

ML Odyssey:

from odyssey.core import zeros, ones, randn, arange, eye

# From values (use full or creation functions)
var x = arange(1.0, 4.0, 1.0, DType.float32)

# Zeros/ones - note explicit dtype
var y = zeros[DType.float32](10, 10)
var z = ones[DType.float32](5, 5)

# Random
var r = randn[DType.float32](3, 4)

# Range
var a = arange(0.0, 10.0, 1.0, DType.float32)

# Identity
var I = eye(3, DType.float32)

Key difference: ML Odyssey requires explicit dtype specification.

docs.title: Basic Arithmetic
docs.text:
PyTorch:

c = a + b
d = a - b
e = a * b  # Element-wise
f = a / b
g = a ** 2

ML Odyssey:

var c = a + b
var d = a - b
var e = a * b  # Element-wise
var f = a / b
var g = a ** 2.0

Operations are nearly identical.

docs.title: Matrix Operations
docs.text:
PyTorch:

# Matrix multiplication
c = a @ b
c = torch.matmul(a, b)
c = torch.mm(a, b)

# Transpose
t = a.T
t = a.transpose(0, 1)

# Dot product
d = torch.dot(a, b)

ML Odyssey:

# Matrix multiplication
var c = a @ b
var c = matmul(a, b)

# Transpose
var t = a.T
var t = a.transpose()

# Dot product
var d = dot(a, b)

docs.title: Shape Operations
docs.text:
PyTorch:

# Reshape
y = x.reshape(2, 3)
y = x.view(2, 3)

# Squeeze/unsqueeze
y = x.squeeze()
y = x.unsqueeze(0)

# Flatten
y = x.flatten()

ML Odyssey:

# Reshape - uses List[Int] for shape
var y = x.reshape(List[Int](2, 3))

# Squeeze/unsqueeze
var y = x.squeeze()
var y = x.unsqueeze(0)

# Flatten
var y = x.flatten()

docs.title: Indexing and Slicing
docs.text:
PyTorch:

# Single element
x[0, 1]

# Row/column
x[0]        # First row
x[:, 1]     # Second column

# Slice
x[0:5, 2:7]

ML Odyssey:

# Single element - uses List[Int]
x[List[Int](0, 1)]

# Row
x[List[Int](0)]

# Slice
var starts = List[Int](0, 2)
var ends = List[Int](5, 7)
x.slice(starts, ends)

# Or use slice_along_axis
x.slice_along_axis(axis=1, start=2, end=7)

Key difference: ML Odyssey uses explicit slice functions instead of Python's slice syntax.

docs.title: Reductions
docs.text:
PyTorch:

# Sum
s = x.sum()
s = x.sum(dim=0)
s = x.sum(dim=1, keepdim=True)

# Mean, max, min
m = x.mean()
mx = x.max()
mn = x.min()

ML Odyssey:

# Sum
var s = x.sum()
var s = x.sum(axis=0)
var s = x.sum(axis=1, keepdim=True)

# Mean, max, min
var m = x.mean()
var mx = x.max()
var mn = x.min()

Nearly identical, but uses axis instead of dim.

docs.title: Linear Layer
docs.text:
PyTorch:

import torch.nn as nn

layer = nn.Linear(784, 128)
output = layer(input)

ML Odyssey:

from odyssey.core.layers import Linear

var layer = Linear(784, 128)
var output = layer.forward(input)

Key difference: ML Odyssey uses explicit .forward() call.

docs.title: Convolutional Layer
docs.text:
PyTorch:

conv = nn.Conv2d(3, 64, kernel_size=3, padding=1)
output = conv(input)  # NCHW format

ML Odyssey:

from odyssey.core.layers import Conv2d

var conv = Conv2d(3, 64, kernel_size=3, padding=1)
var output = conv.forward(input)  # NCHW format

Same format conventions (NCHW).

docs.title: Batch Normalization
docs.text:
PyTorch:

bn = nn.BatchNorm2d(64)
bn.train()  # Training mode
# bn in inference mode for testing

ML Odyssey:

from odyssey.core.layers import BatchNorm2d

var bn = BatchNorm2d(64)
bn.train()  # Training mode
bn.set_inference_mode()  # Inference mode

docs.title: Activation Functions
docs.text:
PyTorch:

import torch.nn.functional as F

y = F.relu(x)
y = F.sigmoid(x)
y = F.softmax(x, dim=-1)
y = torch.tanh(x)

ML Odyssey:

from odyssey.core import relu, sigmoid, softmax, tanh

var y = relu(x)
var y = sigmoid(x)
var y = softmax(x, dim=-1)
var y = tanh(x)

docs.title: PyTorch Model
docs.text:
class SimpleModel(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(784, 128)
        self.relu = nn.ReLU()
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(128, 10)

    def forward(self, x):
        x = self.fc1(x)
        x = self.relu(x)
        x = self.fc2(x)
        return x

model = SimpleModel()

docs.title: ML Odyssey Model
docs.text:
from odyssey.core.layers import Linear, ReLU

struct SimpleModel:
    var fc1: Linear
    var relu: ReLU
    var fc2: Linear

    fn __init__(out self) raises:
        self.fc1 = Linear(784, 128)
        self.relu = ReLU()
        self.fc2 = Linear(128, 10)

    fn forward(mut self, x: AnyTensor) raises -> AnyTensor:
        var out = self.fc1.forward(x)
        out = self.relu.forward(out)
        out = self.fc2.forward(out)
        return out

    fn parameters(self) -> List[AnyTensor]:
        var params = List[AnyTensor]()
        params.extend(self.fc1.parameters())
        params.extend(self.fc2.parameters())
        return params^

var model = SimpleModel()

Key differences:

  1. Use struct instead of class
  2. Use fn __init__(out self) constructor
  3. Explicit .forward() calls
  4. Manual parameters() collection

docs.title: PyTorch (Implicit)
docs.text:
x = torch.tensor([1.0], requires_grad=True)
y = x * 2 + 1
loss = y.sum()

loss.backward()  # Compute gradients
print(x.grad)    # Access gradient

docs.title: ML Odyssey (Explicit Tape)
docs.text:
from odyssey.autograd import Tape

var tape = Tape()
with tape:
    var x = randn[DType.float32](1)
    var y = x * 2.0 + 1.0
    var loss = y.sum()

var grads = tape.backward(loss)
var dx = grads.get(x)  # Access gradient

Key difference: ML Odyssey uses explicit tape recording.

docs.title: Disabling Gradients
docs.text:
PyTorch:

with torch.no_grad():
    output = model(input)

ML Odyssey:

from odyssey.autograd import no_grad

with no_grad():
    var output = model.forward(input)

docs.title: PyTorch
docs.text:
model = SimpleModel()
optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=0.001)
criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()

for epoch in range(10):
    for batch in dataloader:
        optimizer.zero_grad()

        output = model(batch['input'])
        loss = criterion(output, batch['target'])

        loss.backward()
        optimizer.step()

    print(f"Epoch {epoch}, Loss: {loss.item()}")

docs.title: ML Odyssey
docs.text:
from odyssey.training.optimizers import Adam
from odyssey.core.layers import CrossEntropyLoss
from odyssey.autograd import Tape

var model = SimpleModel()
var optimizer = Adam(model.parameters(), lr=0.001)
var criterion = CrossEntropyLoss()

for epoch in range(10):
    for batch in dataloader:
        var tape = Tape()
        with tape:
            var output = model.forward(batch.input)
            var loss = criterion.forward(output, batch.target)

        optimizer.zero_grad()
        var grads = tape.backward(loss)
        optimizer.step()

    print("Epoch", epoch, "Loss:", loss.item[DType.float32]())

docs.title: 1. Explicit DTypes
docs.text:
# PyTorch - dtype inferred
x = torch.tensor([1.0])  # float32 by default

# ML Odyssey - dtype required
var x = full(List[Int](1), 1.0, DType.float32)

docs.title: 2. List Shapes
docs.text:
# PyTorch - tuple shapes
x = torch.zeros(3, 4)
x = x.reshape(2, 6)

# ML Odyssey - List[Int] shapes
var x = zeros[DType.float32](3, 4)  # Variadic OK for creation
var x = x.reshape(List[Int](2, 6))  # List for reshape

docs.title: 3. Parameter Collection
docs.text:
# PyTorch - automatic registration
class Model(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.fc = nn.Linear(10, 5)  # Auto-registered

model.parameters()  # Works automatically

# ML Odyssey - manual collection
struct Model:
    var fc: Linear

    fn parameters(self) -> List[AnyTensor]:
        return self.fc.parameters()  # Must implement

docs.title: 4. In-Place Operations
docs.text:
# PyTorch - common, uses underscore suffix
x.add_(1)
x.relu_()

# ML Odyssey - explicit mutation
x.add_inplace(1)  # Or create new tensor
var y = relu(x)

docs.title: 5. Device Management
docs.text:
# PyTorch
x = x.to('cuda')
model = model.cuda()

# ML Odyssey - CPU only (for now)
# No device management needed

docs.title: Export from PyTorch
docs.text:
import torch
import numpy as np

# Save model weights
model = SimpleModel()
state = model.state_dict()

# Convert to numpy and save
weights = {}
for name, param in state.items():
    weights[name] = param.cpu().numpy()

np.savez('weights.npz', **weights)

docs.title: Load in ML Odyssey
docs.text:
# Load weights (when npz loading is implemented)
var weights = load_npz("weights.npz")
model.load_state_dict(weights)

docs.title: Migration Checklist
docs.text:
• [ ] Replace torch.Tensor with AnyTensor
  • [ ] Add explicit dtype to tensor creation
  • [ ] Replace class with struct for models
  • [ ] Change __call__ to explicit .forward() calls
  • [ ] Implement parameters() method manually
  • [ ] Wrap forward pass in Tape context
  • [ ] Replace dim= with axis= in reductions
  • [ ] Convert slice syntax to .slice() methods
  • [ ] Remove .to(device) calls (CPU only for now)
